href="/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff2" type="font/woff2" crossorigin="anonymous"/><style>
	/* latin */
	@font-face {
		font-family: 'Inter';
		font-style: normal;
		font-weight: 400;
		font-display: swap;
		src: local('Inter Regular'), local('Inter-Regular'),
			url('/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff2') format('woff2'), /* Chrome 26+, Opera 23+, Firefox 39+ */
			url('/static/fonts/inter-regular-optimized.woff') format('woff'); /* Chrome 6+, Firefox 3.6+, IE 9+, Safari 5.1+ */
	}
	/* latin */
	@font-face {
		font-family: 'Inter';
		font-style: normal;
		font-weight: bold;
		font-display: swap;
		src: local('Inter Heavy'), local('Inter-Heavy'),
			url('/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff2') format('woff2'), /* Chrome 26+, Opera 23+, Firefox 39+ */
			url('/static/fonts/inter-bold-optimized.woff') format('woff'); /* Chrome 6+, Firefox 3.6+, IE 9+, Safari 5.1+ */
	}
	* {
		margin: 0;
		padding: 0;
		box-sizing: border-box;
		-webkit-box-sizing: border-box;
	}
	html, body {
		height: 100%;
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		color: #1C2838;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	html {
		height: 100%;
		width: 100%;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: subpixel-antialiased;
	}

	:root {
		--black: #1a1a1a;
		--white: #ffffff;

		--title-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--copy-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--font-base: 16px;
		--font-base-height: 25px;

		--h1-hero-size: 48px;
		--h1-size: 40px;
		--h2-size: 32px;
		--h3-size: 24px;
		--h4-size: 16px;

		—-breakpoint: 992px;
		—-breakpoint-mobile: 420px;
		—-breakpoint-desktop: 1200px;

		--success: #49a147;
		--warning: #ffad0d;
		--error: #e35141;

		--box-shadow: 0px 5px 20px -5px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);
		--box-shadow-100: 0px 1px 3px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15),
		0px 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);

		--gray-100: #f8f8f8;
		--gray-200: #eeeeee;
		--gray-300: #cccccc;
		--gray-400: #6a6a6a;
		--gray-500: #1a1a1a;

		--primary: #1d2758;
		--primary-100: #e4e5eb;
		--primary-200: #8e93ac;
		--primary-300: #1d2758;
		--primary-400: #151d47;


		--secondary: #eff6f7;
		--secondary-100: #E5EBF5;
		--secondary-200: #92ACD3;
		--secondary-300: #2459A9;
		--secondary-400: #163E8F;

		--tertiary: #2459a9;
		--tertiary-100: #FAFCFD;
		--tertiary-200: #F4F9F9;
		--tertiary-300: #EFF6F7;
		--tertiary-400: #E4EFF1;

		--interactive: #c77124;
		--interactive-100: #f8eee5;
		--interactive-200: #e3b892;
		--interactive-300: #c77124;
		--interactive-400: #ba5e1b;

		--accent-200: #F4F9F9;

		--focus: #005FCC;

		--gradient-100: linear-gradient(183.11deg, rgba(248, 238, 229, 0.4) 2.57%, rgba(229, 235, 245, 0.4) 97.42%);
		--gradient-200: linear-gradient(90deg, #EFF6F7 0.02%, #F8EEE5 99.96%);
	}

	h1 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	h2 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h3 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h4 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}

	p, span, a {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
	}
	p {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 25px;
		margin: 26px 0;
	}
	a {
		color: var(--interactive);
	}


	/* Same styles as p but can be used on <div>s */
	.bold{
		font-weight: bold;
	}
	.text-4xl {
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	.text-3xl {
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
	}
	.text-2xl {
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {
		-moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
		display: inline-block;
		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}

Residential","content":"\u003cp\u003eWith \u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/inpatient\"\u003einpatient residential treatment\u003c/a\u003e, patients live in the treatment hospital or facility 24/7 while receiving supervised and structured care. The \u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/how-long-rehab-last\"\u003elength of treatment\u003c/a\u003e varies according to the level of care needed. But options range from a few days to 30-, 60-, or 90-day stays or longer.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Outpatient Treatment","content":"\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/outpatient-treatment\"\u003eOutpatient treatment\u003c/a\u003e doesn’t require overnight stays, so patients live in their own residences and visit hospitals or facilities for \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment\"\u003etreatment\u003c/a\u003e. Standard outpatient programs typically involve fewer than 9 hours per week. Robust outpatient options include partial hospitalization (PHP) (aka day treatment) and \u003ca href=\"/intensive-outpatient-programs\"\u003eintensive outpatient (IOP)\u003c/a\u003e care.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Safe Medical Detox","content":"\u003cp\u003eWhile not always required, \u003ca href=\"/drug-detox\"\u003emedical detox\u003c/a\u003e aims to medically stabilize patients, prevent harmful effects of withdrawal, minimize withdrawal symptoms, and help patients transition to a rehabilitation program or other form of continued care.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Sober Living","content":"\u003cp\u003eOur \u003ca href=\"/sober-living\"\u003esober living facilities\u003c/a\u003e offer drug- and alcohol-free living environments that help residents solidify habits and coping mechanisms before returning to everyday life.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Co-Occurring Disorders","content":"\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"/rehab-guide/substance-use-disorder\"\u003eSubstance use disorders (SUDs)\u003c/a\u003e often occur alongside mental disorders, such as anxiety, depression, post-traumatic stress disorder (PTSD), and more. Our facilities treat the whole person, offering care for both SUDs and \u003ca href=\"/co-occurring-disorders\"\u003eco-occurring disorders\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Specialized Programs","content":"\u003cp\u003eGiven the importance of individualized treatment, we provide a host of programs geared toward various patient groups such as \u003ca href=\"/veterans/specialized-treatment-program\"\u003eVeterans and first responders\u003c/a\u003e and members of the \u003ca href=\"/lgbtqiapk-addiction/lgbtq-drug-addiction-substance-abuse\"\u003eLGBTQ+\u003c/a\u003e community. Plus, AAC provides not only faith-based and 12-step programs such as Alcoholics Anonymous and Narcotics Anonymous but also secular program options. In addition, we offer a special opportunity for \u003ca href=\"https://aacalumni.com/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003ealumni\u003c/a\u003e to connect, support one another, and contribute to the community.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Therapies","content":"\u003cp\u003eGroup, one-on-one, and \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment/family-therapy\"\u003efamily therapies\u003c/a\u003e are available as are trauma-based therapies to help individuals address underlying issues that can contribute to substance misuse and addiction. Specific therapy types include: \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment/cognitive-behavioral-therapy\"\u003ecognitive behavioral therapy\u003c/a\u003e, \u003ca href=\"/addiction-medications\"\u003emedication assisted treatment\u003c/a\u003e, contingency management, \u003ca href=\"/therapy-treatment/motivational-interviewing\"\u003emotivational interviewing\u003c/a\u003e, and much more.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Telehealth","content":"\u003cp\u003eOffered via confidential, HIPAA-compliant video conferences, our outpatient telehealth services provide online treatment support. Services are available to residents of Arizona, Florida, Missouri, Nevada, Pennsylvania, Rhode Island, Texas, Vermont, and Washington.\u003c/p\u003e\n"}],"homepage_customizations":{"link_bank":[{"title":"Paying for Treatment","links":[{"copy":"Payment Options","href":"/payment-options"},{"copy":"Check Your Insurance Coverage","href":"/verify-insurance"},{"copy":"Insurance Provider Information","href":"/insurance-coverage"}]},{"title":"Treatment Resources","links":[{"copy":"Rehab Guide for Addiction","href":"/rehab-guide"},{"copy":"Veteran Treatment Resources ","href":"/veterans"},{"copy":"State-Funded Rehab","href":"/rehab-guide/state-funded"}]},{"title":"FAQs","links":[{"copy":"Our Admission Process","href":"/admissions"},{"copy":"Rehab Cost","href":"/rehab-guide/rehab-cost"},{"copy":"Length of Stay","href":"/rehab-guide/how-long-rehab-last"}]}]}}},"acfOptions":{"acf":{"content_segment_cta_defaults":{"":null,"segment-pr-1":{"value_prop_cta":{"title":"Struggling? EXPERT ADDICTION TREATMENT

15+ Years of Trusted Care

 

At AAC, we treat drug and alcohol addiction along with mental and behavioral health issues at multiple locations nationwide.

 View Our Locations 

 Outpatient Locations 

 

Personalized Support

Not sure where to start? Follow our guided intake process.

 

Our guided online intake process makes it easy to take the first step, whether it’s for you or someone you care about. You'll answer a few simple questions to help us understand your unique situation and connect you with the right treatment options. It’s quick, simple, and personalized to make getting help as easy as possible.

OUR CONTENT POLICY

Editorial Policy & Content Guidelines

We are committed to delivering original, truthful, accurate, unbiased, up-to-date information. We strive to create content that is clear, concise, and easy to understand. Learn more about our policy .

 
 

Our Policy

AAC does not accept any advertisements or sponsorships. Our pages remain true to current best practices, without the influence of sponsorship or advertising.

 
 
 
 

Our Sources

During the editorial and medical review process, our team fact checks information and ensures that we only use reputable, credible, and current sources in our content.

 
 
 
 
 

Our Contributors

Our editors and medical reviewers include AAC staff and industry experts. Our credentialed editors are addiction experts who review content to ensure that it is accurate and current.

FROM PAST PATIENTS

Alumni Testimonials
 View All Testimonials 
 

 
 

 
 

 
Support Built for Veterans

Our specialized program honors your service with care designed for veterans. Many of our staff have served too, and are here to support your recovery.

“Addiction Talk” is an award-winning online talk show featuring the personal stories of celebrities, influencers, and everyday people impacted by addiction.

	* {
		margin: 0;
		padding: 0;
		box-sizing: border-box;
		-webkit-box-sizing: border-box;
	}
	html, body {
		height: 100%;
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		color: #1C2838;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	html {
		height: 100%;
		width: 100%;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: subpixel-antialiased;
	}

	:root {
		--black: #1a1a1a;
		--white: #ffffff;

		--title-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--copy-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--font-base: 16px;
		--font-base-height: 25px;

		--h1-hero-size: 48px;
		--h1-size: 40px;
		--h2-size: 32px;
		--h3-size: 24px;
		--h4-size: 16px;

		—-breakpoint: 992px;
		—-breakpoint-mobile: 420px;
		—-breakpoint-desktop: 1200px;

		--success: #49a147;
		--warning: #ffad0d;
		--error: #e35141;

		--box-shadow: 0px 5px 20px -5px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);
		--box-shadow-100: 0px 1px 3px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15),
		0px 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);

		--gray-100: #f8f8f8;
		--gray-200: #eeeeee;
		--gray-300: #cccccc;
		--gray-400: #6a6a6a;
		--gray-500: #1a1a1a;

		--primary: #1d2758;
		--primary-100: #e4e5eb;
		--primary-200: #8e93ac;
		--primary-300: #1d2758;
		--primary-400: #151d47;


		--secondary: #eff6f7;
		--secondary-100: #E5EBF5;
		--secondary-200: #92ACD3;
		--secondary-300: #2459A9;
		--secondary-400: #163E8F;

		--tertiary: #2459a9;
		--tertiary-100: #FAFCFD;
		--tertiary-200: #F4F9F9;
		--tertiary-300: #EFF6F7;
		--tertiary-400: #E4EFF1;

		--interactive: #c77124;
		--interactive-100: #f8eee5;
		--interactive-200: #e3b892;
		--interactive-300: #c77124;
		--interactive-400: #ba5e1b;

		--accent-200: #F4F9F9;

		--focus: #005FCC;

		--gradient-100: linear-gradient(183.11deg, rgba(248, 238, 229, 0.4) 2.57%, rgba(229, 235, 245, 0.4) 97.42%);
		--gradient-200: linear-gradient(90deg, #EFF6F7 0.02%, #F8EEE5 99.96%);
	}

	h1 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	h2 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h3 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h4 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}

	p, span, a {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
	}
	p {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 25px;
		margin: 26px 0;
	}
	a {
		color: var(--interactive);
	}


	/* Same styles as p but can be used on <div>s */
	.bold{
		font-weight: bold;
	}
	.text-4xl {
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	.text-3xl {
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
	}
	.text-2xl {
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {

		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}

hero__content"><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 hero__content--copy">Yes, Suboxone treatment may be covered by your insurance provider. Medication-assisted treatment (MAT) is the standard of care for treating opioid use disorders. Access to medications such as buprenorphine has proven to be a critical component in managing the opioid addiction crisis our country continues to face. As a special formulation of buprenorphine, Suboxone has contributed to many peoples’ recovery from opioid use disorders.<sup>1</sup></div></div></div><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 fill="#fff"></path></svg></div></div></span></span></div></div></div></div></div></section><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 wp-content__container"><div class="jsx-3337807827"><section class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 section__content"><div id="wordpress-content" class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732"><div><h2>Which Health Insurance Plans Cover Suboxone?</h2>
<p>Suboxone doctors, clinics, and providers do accept many forms of health insurance, which may offer full or partial <a href="/insurance-coverage">coverage for Suboxone treatment</a>. Common health insurance companies that cover the cost of Suboxone treatment include (but are not limited to):</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/aetna" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Aetna</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/anthem" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Anthem</a> (Elevance Health in California and Nevada).</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/blue-cross-blue-shield-association" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Blue Cross Blue Shield</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/tricare" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">TRICARE Insuance</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/humana" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Humana</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/kaiser-permanente" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">Kaiser</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/unitedhealth-group" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">UnitedHealth Group</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/using-medicaid-to-pay-for-rehab">Medicaid</a>.</li>
</ul>
<p class="FirstParagraph"><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/suboxone/treatment-center-clinic-near-me">Suboxone clinic</a> fees may vary based on the provider, the location, and the patient.</p>
<div class="portal_insurance-addiction-search" id="portal_insurance-addiction-search-0"></div>
<p><em>American Addiction Centers strives to provide you with the most updated information on each carriers’ addiction insurance coverage, but policy changes and errors do occur. Please check with your insurance carrier directly to confirm coverage levels.</em></p>
</div></div></section><section class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732 section__content-tabs"><div class="sc-67a619c-0 hKbQMs"><div class="accordion  is-expanded"><div class="accordion__head"><div class="accordion__title">What is Suboxone</div></div><div class="accordion__body" style="max-height:auto;overflow-y:auto"><div class="accordion__wrapper"><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732"><h2>What is Suboxone?</h2>
<p><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/suboxone">Suboxone</a> is FDA-approved for use in medication-assisted treatment of opioid use disorders.<sup>2,5 </sup>Suboxone is a combination of buprenorphine (a long-acting partial opioid agonist) and naloxone (an opioid receptor antagonist) and can be used to stabilize people during detox and withdrawal management as well as for longer-term maintenance therapy for opioid use disorders.<sup>2,6,7,8</sup> Like other opioid agonist drugs, buprenorphine functions by binding to the body’s opioid receptors; however, as only a partial agonist at these receptors, buprenorphine elicits relatively less pronounced opioid effects when used within prescribed guidelines.<sup>5,6</sup></p>
<p>With a strong binding affinity for opioid receptors, buprenorphine can also competitively prohibit other illicit and prescription opioids from attaching to these receptors, blocking them from having their reinforcing euphoric effects on the user to discourage continued misuse while on Suboxone treatment.<sup>2</sup> Buprenorphine also has a ceiling to its opioid effects, which lowers the risk of overdose.<sup>2,5</sup></p>
<p>Keep in mind that Suboxone does have the potential to cause euphoria in some people. However, this euphoric potential is generally much milder than that of other opioids, such as heroin. <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/addiction-medications/naloxone">Naloxone</a> has no effect when Suboxone is taken as directed (i.e., oral routes), but it is included in the formulation as an abuse deterrent, as intentional misuse—such as attempts to dissolve the Suboxone to be injected—can result in the onset of unpleasant withdrawal symptoms.<sup>2,5,6,7 </sup></p>
<h2>Why do People Use Suboxone?</h2>
<p>People utilize Suboxone as one component in a more comprehensive treatment plan to minimize uncomfortable opioid withdrawal symptoms and control opioid cravings.<sup> </sup>By managing withdrawal and controlling cravings, Suboxone can help a person to remain focused on their recovery and prevent them from relapsing. Suboxone has been shown to be effective at helping people remain abstinent from opioids and improve their treatment outcomes.<sup>2,5,7,9</sup></p>
<h2>What is Suboxone Used For?</h2>
<p><img loading="lazy" decoding="async" class=" wp-image-57936 alignright" src="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1.jpg" alt="Suboxone treatment cost" width="401" height="267" srcset="https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1.jpg 1500w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1-300x200.jpg 300w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1-768x513.jpg 768w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1-1024x683.jpg 1024w" sizes="(max-width: 401px) 100vw, 401px" /></p>
<p>Suboxone is a medication used to treat opioid use disorder in the United States. As part of an effective MAT strategy, Suboxone may be used as a part of a larger treatment plan that includes behavioral therapeutic interventions, monitoring, and support, and mental health care (if needed).<sup> </sup>This ensures a whole-person approach to treatment rather than simply addressing one aspect of a substance use disorder.<sup>5,7,8,9</sup></p>
<p>It is important to wait until you have been abstinent from opioids long enough to be experiencing withdrawal symptoms before starting to take Suboxone. This generally means staying off opioids for at least 12 hours and up to 48 hours, depending on the type of opioids you were using, since taking Suboxone while you are under the influence of opioids can make withdrawal symptoms begin earlier than they normally would.<sup> </sup>Using Suboxone as a long-term maintenance medication encourages you to stay abstinent from opioids, especially since this medication reduces cravings and decreases the potentially rewarding effects of other opioids if you do relapse.<sup>2,5,9,11</sup> Withdrawal symptoms can be extremely uncomfortable and make it difficult to stop using opioids. Suboxone film or tablets can ease many symptoms of opioid withdrawal. Opioid withdrawal symptoms include:<sup>8,11</sup></p>
<ul>
<li>Body aches.</li>
<li>Anxiety.</li>
<li>Inability to sleep.</li>
<li>Agitation.</li>
<li>Sweating.</li>
<li>Teary eyes.</li>
<li>Runny nose.</li>
<li>Goosebumps.</li>
<li>Yawning.</li>
<li>Stomach pain, nausea, vomiting.</li>
<li>Diarrhea.</li>
</ul>
<h2>Preferences for Suboxone</h2>
<p>There are several different MAT options available to people in the United States, but people may choose Suboxone over other forms of MAT for various reasons. A range of factors can influence a person’s decision to opt for treatment using Suboxone rather than <a href="/addiction-medications/methadone">methadone</a> or naltrexone.</p>
<div class="c-callout c-callout--borders--all">
		
		<p>For example, Suboxone tends to offer:<sup>2,5,9,11</sup></p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Fewer restrictions.</strong> In some instances, Suboxone may be more readily accessible than methadone, which is only dispensed in hospital and certified clinic settings. Medical professionals who have been authorized to provide Suboxone can prescribe it in a greater range of settings, including from their office.</li>
<li><strong>Lower misuse potential</strong>. Suboxone is formulated to discourage misuse and diversion. The opioid effects are relatively weaker than full opioid agonist drugs, and a person is unlikely to experience pronounced feelings or euphoria or intoxication from taking Suboxone, when used as prescribed for the treatment of opioid use disorder. If it is taken differently than prescribed, such as snorted or injected, it may cause immediate withdrawal due to the presence of naloxone in the medication.</li>
<li><strong>Increased safety.</strong> The risk of overdose on methadone is more than 4 times higher than on Suboxone. It is unlikely that a person will experience an overdose on Suboxone unless it’s combined with other central nervous system depressants like alcohol or benzodiazepines. Buprenorphine may be safer for pregnant and breastfeeding women to use than methadone.</li>
</ul>
<p>

	</div>
<p>Studies show that buprenorphine, not just the Suboxone formula, has positive recovery outcomes. While it can be difficult to track progress over long periods of time, research shows that people who take buprenorphine or methadone are more likely at 18 months to remain abstinent, maintain employment, and engage in <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/therapy-treatment/aftercare-support-groups" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">12-step or mutual help meetings to support their recovery</a>. Buprenorphine is associated with a lower likelihood of dropping out of treatment, relapse, overdose deaths, and improved quality of life vs. methadone.<sup>2,9</sup> Opioid maintenance on buprenorphine also is associated with a lower risk of contracting infectious diseases like HIV or hepatitis C, and having new legal issues. In one study, the majority of people, 75% on buprenorphine were successful in their recovery attempts.<sup>9</sup></p>
</div></div></div></div></div><div class="sc-67a619c-0 hKbQMs"><div class="accordion  "><div class="accordion__head"><div class="accordion__title">Suboxone Costs</div></div><div class="accordion__body" style="max-height:auto;overflow-y:auto"><div class="accordion__wrapper"><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732"><h2>How Much is Suboxone Without Insurance?</h2>
<p>The cost of Suboxone depends on a variety of factors. Different insurance companies offer varying levels of coverage, and not all insurance plans provide the same level of coverage for treatments or medications. Suboxone costs can be different depending on the provider that you obtain your prescription from, whether it is through a doctor’s office, an inpatient facility, or an outpatient clinic.</p>
<p>Suboxone’s cost without insurance depends on whether you receive the brand name or generic version of Suboxone, as well as your dosage. However, receiving Suboxone treatment without insurance may involve higher out-of-pocket costs than using health insurance to cover part or all of your Suboxone treatment.</p>
<h3>Does Medicaid Cover Suboxone?</h3>
<p>Medicaid generally does cover the Suboxone cost for treatment of opioid use disorder, but the extent of coverage depends on various factors. Suboxone is listed on the preferred drug list for Medicaid in all but one state, although the majority of these states require prior authorization.<sup>7</sup></p>
<p>Prior authorization means that the medical professional prescribing Suboxone has to obtain approval from Medicaid before the medication will be approved, and may have to submit documentation showing that it is medically necessary, or that the person is receiving counseling in addition to medication.<sup> </sup>Medicaid imposes limits on how long a person can stay on Suboxone, although the limit depends on your state and some states set lifetime limits. If you exceed this lifetime limit, coverage for Suboxone may be denied through Medicaid.<sup>7</sup></p>
<h2>Is There Cost Assistance for Suboxone?</h2>
<p>Two cost-assistance programs are available for Suboxone, depending on whether you have private health insurance or no health insurance coverage. A cost-assistance program is when part or all of the cost of the medication is paid for by another source, making it more affordable. If you have private health insurance, a copay assistance program is available that reduces your copay by up to $75 monthly.<sup> </sup>If you have no health insurance, the cost assistance program provides a discount card that offers a discount on your prescriptions, depending on the dosage.<sup>13 </sup> You can learn more about the INSUPPORT program <a href="https://www.insupport.com/suboxone/savings" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">here</a>.</p>
</div></div></div></div></div><div class="sc-67a619c-0 hKbQMs"><div class="accordion  "><div class="accordion__head"><div class="accordion__title">Getting Suboxone</div></div><div class="accordion__body" style="max-height:auto;overflow-y:auto"><div class="accordion__wrapper"><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-3838467732"><h2>How do I Get Suboxone?</h2>
<p>To obtain a prescription for Suboxone, you must find a qualified practitioner who is specially-wavered to prescribe and dispense the medication.<sup>2,7</sup> This practitioner waiver and a modified DEA registration number are provided upon completion of a training program, allowing a provider to prescribe Suboxone.<sup>5,7,11</sup> You may be able to get Suboxone by visiting your family doctor, provided that they possess a buprenorphine waiver. There is nothing to be ashamed of by asking your family doctor if they can give you a prescription for Suboxone, or discussing the fact that you struggle with opioid addiction. Talking honestly with your doctor can help them direct you towards the most appropriate treatment to meet your needs.</p>
<p>You can also get a prescription for Suboxone by going to a rehabilitation center or Suboxone clinic that is qualified to treat patients with Suboxone and other forms of MAT.<sup>5</sup> American Addiction Centers has a full range of treatment programs, from detox to inpatient care to outpatient care, providing the highest quality treatment across the country. You can receive Suboxone at American Addiction Centers facilities while receiving evidence-based treatment to address your opioid use disorder.<sup>14,15</sup></p>
<p>For more information on how and where to attain Suboxone for the treatment of your opioid use disorder, call American Addiction Centers at <span class='portal_phone_link' id='portal_phone_link-69a7537b6357b'><script crossorigin type='application/json'>{"props":{"helpline":"true"},"children":""}</script></span></p>
<h2>Find an MAT Rehab Center Near Me</h2>

<h2>Who Can Prescribe Suboxone?</h2>
<p>Suboxone is available through a range of providers and settings. SAMHSA-certified opioid treatment programs are able to prescribe Suboxone to their patients. Additionally, various qualified providers who are able to prescribe medications can write prescriptions for Suboxone, making this medication much more accessible than some other forms of MAT.<sup>2,5,11,15 </sup>You can get Suboxone prescribed by a wavered doctor, physician assistant, or nurse practitioner.<sup>2,7,15</sup></p>
<h2>What Substance Addiction Does Health Insurance Cover?</h2>
<p>Many major health insurance providers cover treatment for the various substance use disorders, including addictions to alcohol, cocaine, and heroin. Attending <a href="/insurance-coverage" target="_blank" rel="noopener noreferrer">rehab for drug addiction</a> can also address when you have an issue with multiple substances.</p>

He received his medical degree from the University of California, San Diego School of Medicine.","post_status":"publish","comment_status":"closed","ping_status":"closed","post_password":"","post_name":"scot-thomas","to_ping":"","pinged":"","post_modified":"2025-05-28 17:18:31","post_modified_gmt":"2025-05-28 22:18:31","post_content_filtered":"","post_parent":0,"guid":"https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/?post_type=contributor\u0026#038;p=54389","menu_order":0,"post_type":"contributor","post_mime_type":"","comment_count":"0","filter":"raw"},"content_tabs":[{"title":"What is Suboxone","content":"\u003ch2\u003eWhat is Suboxone?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/suboxone\"\u003eSuboxone\u003c/a\u003e is FDA-approved for use in medication-assisted treatment of opioid use disorders.\u003csup\u003e2,5 \u003c/sup\u003eSuboxone is a combination of buprenorphine (a long-acting partial opioid agonist) and naloxone (an opioid receptor antagonist) and can be used to stabilize people during detox and withdrawal management as well as for longer-term maintenance therapy for opioid use disorders.\u003csup\u003e2,6,7,8\u003c/sup\u003e Like other opioid agonist drugs, buprenorphine functions by binding to the body’s opioid receptors; however, as only a partial agonist at these receptors, buprenorphine elicits relatively less pronounced opioid effects when used within prescribed guidelines.\u003csup\u003e5,6\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eWith a strong binding affinity for opioid receptors, buprenorphine can also competitively prohibit other illicit and prescription opioids from attaching to these receptors, blocking them from having their reinforcing euphoric effects on the user to discourage continued misuse while on Suboxone treatment.\u003csup\u003e2\u003c/sup\u003e Buprenorphine also has a ceiling to its opioid effects, which lowers the risk of overdose.\u003csup\u003e2,5\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eKeep in mind that Suboxone does have the potential to cause euphoria in some people. However, this euphoric potential is generally much milder than that of other opioids, such as heroin. \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/addiction-medications/naloxone\"\u003eNaloxone\u003c/a\u003e has no effect when Suboxone is taken as directed (i.e., oral routes), but it is included in the formulation as an abuse deterrent, as intentional misuse—such as attempts to dissolve the Suboxone to be injected—can result in the onset of unpleasant withdrawal symptoms.\u003csup\u003e2,5,6,7 \u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003eWhy do People Use Suboxone?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003ePeople utilize Suboxone as one component in a more comprehensive treatment plan to minimize uncomfortable opioid withdrawal symptoms and control opioid cravings.\u003csup\u003e \u003c/sup\u003eBy managing withdrawal and controlling cravings, Suboxone can help a person to remain focused on their recovery and prevent them from relapsing. Suboxone has been shown to be effective at helping people remain abstinent from opioids and improve their treatment outcomes.\u003csup\u003e2,5,7,9\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003eWhat is Suboxone Used For?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003cimg loading=\"lazy\" decoding=\"async\" class=\" wp-image-57936 alignright\" src=\"https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1.jpg\" alt=\"Suboxone treatment cost\" width=\"401\" height=\"267\" srcset=\"https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1.jpg 1500w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1-300x200.jpg 300w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1-768x513.jpg 768w, https://admin.americanaddictioncenters.org/wp-content/uploads/2019/05/use-of-Suboxone-1-1024x683.jpg 1024w\" sizes=\"(max-width: 401px) 100vw, 401px\" /\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSuboxone is a medication used to treat opioid use disorder in the United States. As part of an effective MAT strategy, Suboxone may be used as a part of a larger treatment plan that includes behavioral therapeutic interventions, monitoring, and support, and mental health care (if needed).\u003csup\u003e \u003c/sup\u003eThis ensures a whole-person approach to treatment rather than simply addressing one aspect of a substance use disorder.\u003csup\u003e5,7,8,9\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIt is important to wait until you have been abstinent from opioids long enough to be experiencing withdrawal symptoms before starting to take Suboxone. This generally means staying off opioids for at least 12 hours and up to 48 hours, depending on the type of opioids you were using, since taking Suboxone while you are under the influence of opioids can make withdrawal symptoms begin earlier than they normally would.\u003csup\u003e \u003c/sup\u003eUsing Suboxone as a long-term maintenance medication encourages you to stay abstinent from opioids, especially since this medication reduces cravings and decreases the potentially rewarding effects of other opioids if you do relapse.\u003csup\u003e2,5,9,11\u003c/sup\u003e Withdrawal symptoms can be extremely uncomfortable and make it difficult to stop using opioids. Suboxone film or tablets can ease many symptoms of opioid withdrawal. Opioid withdrawal symptoms include:\u003csup\u003e8,11\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eBody aches.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eAnxiety.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eInability to sleep.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eAgitation.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eSweating.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eTeary eyes.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eRunny nose.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eGoosebumps.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eYawning.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eStomach pain, nausea, vomiting.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003eDiarrhea.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003ePreferences for Suboxone\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThere are several different MAT options available to people in the United States, but people may choose Suboxone over other forms of MAT for various reasons. A range of factors can influence a person’s decision to opt for treatment using Suboxone rather than \u003ca href=\"/addiction-medications/methadone\"\u003emethadone\u003c/a\u003e or naltrexone.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cdiv class=\"c-callout c-callout--borders--all\"\u003e\n\t\t\n\t\t\u003cp\u003eFor example, Suboxone tends to offer:\u003csup\u003e2,5,9,11\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eFewer restrictions.\u003c/strong\u003e In some instances, Suboxone may be more readily accessible than methadone, which is only dispensed in hospital and certified clinic settings. Medical professionals who have been authorized to provide Suboxone can prescribe it in a greater range of settings, including from their office.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eLower misuse potential\u003c/strong\u003e. Suboxone is formulated to discourage misuse and diversion. The opioid effects are relatively weaker than full opioid agonist drugs, and a person is unlikely to experience pronounced feelings or euphoria or intoxication from taking Suboxone, when used as prescribed for the treatment of opioid use disorder. If it is taken differently than prescribed, such as snorted or injected, it may cause immediate withdrawal due to the presence of naloxone in the medication.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eIncreased safety.\u003c/strong\u003e The risk of overdose on methadone is more than 4 times higher than on Suboxone. It is unlikely that a person will experience an overdose on Suboxone unless it’s combined with other central nervous system depressants like alcohol or benzodiazepines. Buprenorphine may be safer for pregnant and breastfeeding women to use than methadone.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\n\n\t\u003c/div\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eStudies show that buprenorphine, not just the Suboxone formula, has positive recovery outcomes. While it can be difficult to track progress over long periods of time, research shows that people who take buprenorphine or methadone are more likely at 18 months to remain abstinent, maintain employment, and engage in \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/therapy-treatment/aftercare-support-groups\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"\u003e12-step or mutual help meetings to support their recovery\u003c/a\u003e. Buprenorphine is associated with a lower likelihood of dropping out of treatment, relapse, overdose deaths, and improved quality of life vs. methadone.\u003csup\u003e2,9\u003c/sup\u003e Opioid maintenance on buprenorphine also is associated with a lower risk of contracting infectious diseases like HIV or hepatitis C, and having new legal issues. In one study, the majority of people, 75% on buprenorphine were successful in their recovery attempts.\u003csup\u003e9\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Suboxone Costs","content":"\u003ch2\u003eHow Much is Suboxone Without Insurance?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe cost of Suboxone depends on a variety of factors. Different insurance companies offer varying levels of coverage, and not all insurance plans provide the same level of coverage for treatments or medications. Suboxone costs can be different depending on the provider that you obtain your prescription from, whether it is through a doctor’s office, an inpatient facility, or an outpatient clinic.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSuboxone’s cost without insurance depends on whether you receive the brand name or generic version of Suboxone, as well as your dosage. However, receiving Suboxone treatment without insurance may involve higher out-of-pocket costs than using health insurance to cover part or all of your Suboxone treatment.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch3\u003eDoes Medicaid Cover Suboxone?\u003c/h3\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eMedicaid generally does cover the Suboxone cost for treatment of opioid use disorder, but the extent of coverage depends on various factors. Suboxone is listed on the preferred drug list for Medicaid in all but one state, although the majority of these states require prior authorization.\u003csup\u003e7\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003ePrior authorization means that the medical professional prescribing Suboxone has to obtain approval from Medicaid before the medication will be approved, and may have to submit documentation showing that it is medically necessary, or that the person is receiving counseling in addition to medication.\u003csup\u003e \u003c/sup\u003eMedicaid imposes limits on how long a person can stay on Suboxone, although the limit depends on your state and some states set lifetime limits. If you exceed this lifetime limit, coverage for Suboxone may be denied through Medicaid.\u003csup\u003e7\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003eIs There Cost Assistance for Suboxone?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eTwo cost-assistance programs are available for Suboxone, depending on whether you have private health insurance or no health insurance coverage. A cost-assistance program is when part or all of the cost of the medication is paid for by another source, making it more affordable. If you have private health insurance, a copay assistance program is available that reduces your copay by up to $75 monthly.\u003csup\u003e \u003c/sup\u003eIf you have no health insurance, the cost assistance program provides a discount card that offers a discount on your prescriptions, depending on the dosage.\u003csup\u003e13 \u003c/sup\u003e You can learn more about the INSUPPORT program \u003ca href=\"https://www.insupport.com/suboxone/savings\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"\u003ehere\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/p\u003e\n"},{"title":"Getting Suboxone","content":"\u003ch2\u003eHow do I Get Suboxone?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eTo obtain a prescription for Suboxone, you must find a qualified practitioner who is specially-wavered to prescribe and dispense the medication.\u003csup\u003e2,7\u003c/sup\u003e This practitioner waiver and a modified DEA registration number are provided upon completion of a training program, allowing a provider to prescribe Suboxone.\u003csup\u003e5,7,11\u003c/sup\u003e You may be able to get Suboxone by visiting your family doctor, provided that they possess a buprenorphine waiver. There is nothing to be ashamed of by asking your family doctor if they can give you a prescription for Suboxone, or discussing the fact that you struggle with opioid addiction. Talking honestly with your doctor can help them direct you towards the most appropriate treatment to meet your needs.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eYou can also get a prescription for Suboxone by going to a rehabilitation center or Suboxone clinic that is qualified to treat patients with Suboxone and other forms of MAT.\u003csup\u003e5\u003c/sup\u003e American Addiction Centers has a full range of treatment programs, from detox to inpatient care to outpatient care, providing the highest quality treatment across the country. You can receive Suboxone at American Addiction Centers facilities while receiving evidence-based treatment to address your opioid use disorder.\u003csup\u003e14,15\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eFor more information on how and where to attain Suboxone for the treatment of your opioid use disorder, call American Addiction Centers at \u003cspan class='portal_phone_link' id='portal_phone_link-69a7537b6357b'\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin Suboxone?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSuboxone is available through a range of providers and settings. SAMHSA-certified opioid treatment programs are able to prescribe Suboxone to their patients. Additionally, various qualified providers who are able to prescribe medications can write prescriptions for Suboxone, making this medication much more accessible than some other forms of MAT.\u003csup\u003e2,5,11,15 \u003c/sup\u003eYou can get Suboxone prescribed by a wavered doctor, physician assistant, or nurse practitioner.\u003csup\u003e2,7,15\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003eWhat Substance Addiction Does Health Insurance Cover?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eMany major health insurance providers cover treatment for the various substance use disorders, including addictions to alcohol, cocaine, and heroin. Attending \u003ca href=\"/insurance-coverage\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener noreferrer\"\u003erehab for drug addiction\u003c/a\u003e can also address when you have an issue with multiple Medication-assisted treatment (MAT) is the standard of care for treating opioid use disorders. Access to medications such as buprenorphine has proven to be a critical component in managing the opioid addiction crisis our country continues to face. As a special formulation of buprenorphine, Suboxone has contributed to many peoples’ recovery from opioid use disorders.\u003csup\u003e1\u003c/sup\u003e","ssvob_section_content":"\u003cp style=\"text-align: left;\"\u003eSuboxone is an effective form of medication-assisted treatment that has contributed to many peoples’ recovery from \u003ca href=\"/opioids\"\u003eopioid use disorders\u003c/a\u003e.\u003csup\u003e2 \u003c/sup\u003eRead on to learn more about Suboxone and the financial factors associated with Suboxone treatment. American Addiction Centers (AAC) offers various forms of \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/addiction-medications\"\u003emedication-assisted treatment\u003c/a\u003e, including Suboxone, at our treatment centers across the United States. We’re also in-network with various insurance companies, such as Aetna, Blue Cross Blue Shield, United Healthcare, and more.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp style=\"text-align: right;\"\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/suboxone/esp\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"alternate noopener noreferrer\" hreflang=\"es\"\u003eSpanish Version\u003c/a\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2 style=\"text-align: left;\"\u003eIs Suboxone Covered by Insurance?\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp style=\"text-align: left;\"\u003eYes, the majority of health insurers do generally cover the cost of Suboxone treatment.\u003csup\u003e3\u003c/sup\u003e However, whether your Suboxone treatment will be covered depends on your specific insurance plan and the particular Suboxone provider you choose.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp style=\"text-align: left;\"\u003eSince the passing of the Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act, most health insurance companies that offer mental health and substance use benefits are required to provide coverage that is equivalent to the benefits that are offered for physical health conditions.\u003csup\u003e3 \u003c/sup\u003eThis has worked to improve access to addiction treatment and medications like Suboxone.\u003csup\u003e4\u003c/sup\u003e Treatment for addiction and mental health is now viewed as an essential health Suboxone Insurance Coverage & Cost Guide (With & Without Insurance)

 
 
 
 Medical Detox 
 Insurance Accepted 
 Same Day Admission 

 Written by: Leah Miller, MHC 

 Edited by: Amelia Sharp 

 Reviewed by: Scot Thomas, MD 

Updated May 24, 2024

Yes, Suboxone treatment may be covered by your insurance provider. Medication-assisted treatment (MAT) is the standard of care for treating opioid use disorders. Access to medications such as buprenorphine has proven to be a critical component in managing the opioid addiction crisis our country continues to face. As a special formulation of buprenorphine, Suboxone has contributed to many peoples’ recovery from opioid use disorders. 1 

 Step 1 /4 Check Coverage 

* These fields are required
 

 Find Insurance Carrier * 

Aetna

 
 
 

 Policy Membership ID 

 WXY1030Z0 
 
 
 
 

 

 Next 

 

 
 Your information is secure & protected by HIPAA 

 

 
 

 

Which Health Insurance Plans Cover Suboxone?

Suboxone doctors, clinics, and providers do accept many forms of health insurance, which may offer full or partial coverage for Suboxone treatment . Common health insurance companies that cover the cost of Suboxone treatment include (but are not limited to):

 

 Aetna .

 Anthem (Elevance Health in California and Nevada).

 Blue Cross Blue Shield .

 TRICARE Insuance .

 Humana .

 Kaiser .

 UnitedHealth Group .

 Medicaid .

 

 Suboxone clinic fees may vary based on the provider, the location, and the patient.

 American Addiction Centers strives to provide you with the most updated information on each carriers’ addiction insurance coverage, but policy changes and errors do occur. Please check with your insurance carrier directly to confirm coverage levels. 

What is Suboxone

What is Suboxone?

 Suboxone is FDA-approved for use in medication-assisted treatment of opioid use disorders. 2,5 Suboxone is a combination of buprenorphine (a long-acting partial opioid agonist) and naloxone (an opioid receptor antagonist) and can be used to stabilize people during detox and withdrawal management as well as for longer-term maintenance therapy for opioid use disorders. 2,6,7,8  Like other opioid agonist drugs, buprenorphine functions by binding to the body’s opioid receptors; however, as only a partial agonist at these receptors, buprenorphine elicits relatively less pronounced opioid effects when used within prescribed guidelines. 5,6 

With a strong binding affinity for opioid receptors, buprenorphine can also competitively prohibit other illicit and prescription opioids from attaching to these receptors, blocking them from having their reinforcing euphoric effects on the user to discourage continued misuse while on Suboxone treatment. 2 Buprenorphine also has a ceiling to its opioid effects, which lowers the risk of overdose. 2,5 

Keep in mind that Suboxone does have the potential to cause euphoria in some people. However, this euphoric potential is generally much milder than that of other opioids, such as heroin. Naloxone has no effect when Suboxone is taken as directed (i.e., oral routes), but it is included in the formulation as an abuse deterrent, as intentional misuse—such as attempts to dissolve the Suboxone to be injected—can result in the onset of unpleasant withdrawal symptoms. 2,5,6,7  

Why do People Use Suboxone?

People utilize Suboxone as one component in a more comprehensive treatment plan to minimize uncomfortable opioid withdrawal symptoms and control opioid cravings.   By managing withdrawal and controlling cravings, Suboxone can help a person to remain focused on their recovery and prevent them from relapsing. Suboxone has been shown to be effective at helping people remain abstinent from opioids and improve their treatment outcomes. 2,5,7,9 

What is Suboxone Used For?

 

Suboxone is a medication used to treat opioid use disorder in the United States. As part of an effective MAT strategy, Suboxone may be used as a part of a larger treatment plan that includes behavioral therapeutic interventions, monitoring, and support, and mental health care (if needed).   This ensures a whole-person approach to treatment rather than simply addressing one aspect of a substance use disorder. 5,7,8,9 

It is important to wait until you have been abstinent from opioids long enough to be experiencing withdrawal symptoms before starting to take Suboxone. This generally means staying off opioids for at least 12 hours and up to 48 hours, depending on the type of opioids you were using, since taking Suboxone while you are under the influence of opioids can make withdrawal symptoms begin earlier than they normally would.   Using Suboxone as a long-term maintenance medication encourages you to stay abstinent from opioids, especially since this medication reduces cravings and decreases the potentially rewarding effects of other opioids if you do relapse. 2,5,9,11  Withdrawal symptoms can be extremely uncomfortable and make it difficult to stop using opioids. Suboxone film or tablets can ease many symptoms of opioid withdrawal. Opioid withdrawal symptoms include: 8,11 

 

Body aches.

Anxiety.

Inability to sleep.

Agitation.

Sweating.

Teary eyes.

Runny nose.

Goosebumps.

Yawning.

Stomach pain, nausea, vomiting.

Diarrhea.

 

Preferences for Suboxone

There are several different MAT options available to people in the United States, but people may choose Suboxone over other forms of MAT for various reasons. A range of factors can influence a person’s decision to opt for treatment using Suboxone rather than methadone or naltrexone.

 
 
For example, Suboxone tends to offer: 2,5,9,11 

 

 Fewer restrictions. In some instances, Suboxone may be more readily accessible than methadone, which is only dispensed in hospital and certified clinic settings. Medical professionals who have been authorized to provide Suboxone can prescribe it in a greater range of settings, including from their office.

 Lower misuse potential . Suboxone is formulated to discourage misuse and diversion. The opioid effects are relatively weaker than full opioid agonist drugs, and a person is unlikely to experience pronounced feelings or euphoria or intoxication from taking Suboxone, when used as prescribed for the treatment of opioid use disorder. If it is taken differently than prescribed, such as snorted or injected, it may cause immediate withdrawal due to the presence of naloxone in the medication.

 Increased safety. The risk of overdose on methadone is more than 4 times higher than on Suboxone. It is unlikely that a person will experience an overdose on Suboxone unless it’s combined with other central nervous system depressants like alcohol or benzodiazepines. Buprenorphine may be safer for pregnant and breastfeeding women to use than methadone.

 

 

Studies show that buprenorphine, not just the Suboxone formula, has positive recovery outcomes. While it can be difficult to track progress over long periods of time, research shows that people who take buprenorphine or methadone are more likely at 18 months to remain abstinent, maintain employment, and engage in 12-step or mutual help meetings to support their recovery . Buprenorphine is associated with a lower likelihood of dropping out of treatment, relapse, overdose deaths, and improved quality of life vs. methadone. 2,9 Opioid maintenance on buprenorphine also is associated with a lower risk of contracting infectious diseases like HIV or hepatitis C, and having new legal issues. In one study, the majority of people, 75% on buprenorphine were successful in their recovery attempts. 9 

Suboxone Costs

How Much is Suboxone Without Insurance?

The cost of Suboxone depends on a variety of factors. Different insurance companies offer varying levels of coverage, and not all insurance plans provide the same level of coverage for treatments or medications. Suboxone costs can be different depending on the provider that you obtain your prescription from, whether it is through a doctor’s office, an inpatient facility, or an outpatient clinic.

Suboxone’s cost without insurance depends on whether you receive the brand name or generic version of Suboxone, as well as your dosage. However, receiving Suboxone treatment without insurance may involve higher out-of-pocket costs than using health insurance to cover part or all of your Suboxone treatment.

Does Medicaid Cover Suboxone?

Medicaid generally does cover the Suboxone cost for treatment of opioid use disorder, but the extent of coverage depends on various factors. Suboxone is listed on the preferred drug list for Medicaid in all but one state, although the majority of these states require prior authorization. 7 

Prior authorization means that the medical professional prescribing Suboxone has to obtain approval from Medicaid before the medication will be approved, and may have to submit documentation showing that it is medically necessary, or that the person is receiving counseling in addition to medication.   Medicaid imposes limits on how long a person can stay on Suboxone, although the limit depends on your state and some states set lifetime limits. If you exceed this lifetime limit, coverage for Suboxone may be denied through Medicaid. 7 

Is There Cost Assistance for Suboxone?

Two cost-assistance programs are available for Suboxone, depending on whether you have private health insurance or no health insurance coverage. A cost-assistance program is when part or all of the cost of the medication is paid for by another source, making it more affordable. If you have private health insurance, a copay assistance program is available that reduces your copay by up to $75 monthly.   If you have no health insurance, the cost assistance program provides a discount card that offers a discount on your prescriptions, depending on the dosage. 13  You can learn more about the INSUPPORT program here .

Getting Suboxone

How do I Get Suboxone?

To obtain a prescription for Suboxone, you must find a qualified practitioner who is specially-wavered to prescribe and dispense the medication. 2,7  This practitioner waiver and a modified DEA registration number are provided upon completion of a training program, allowing a provider to prescribe Suboxone. 5,7,11 You may be able to get Suboxone by visiting your family doctor, provided that they possess a buprenorphine waiver. There is nothing to be ashamed of by asking your family doctor if they can give you a prescription for Suboxone, or discussing the fact that you struggle with opioid addiction. Talking honestly with your doctor can help them direct you towards the most appropriate treatment to meet your needs.

You can also get a prescription for Suboxone by going to a rehabilitation center or Suboxone clinic that is qualified to treat patients with Suboxone and other forms of MAT. 5 American Addiction Centers has a full range of treatment programs, from detox to inpatient care to outpatient care, providing the highest quality treatment across the country. You can receive Suboxone at American Addiction Centers facilities while receiving evidence-based treatment to address your opioid use disorder. 14,15 

For more information on how and where to attain Suboxone for the treatment of your opioid use disorder, call American Addiction Centers at 

Find an MAT Rehab Center Near Me

 

 

Who Can Prescribe Suboxone?

Suboxone is available through a range of providers and settings. SAMHSA-certified opioid treatment programs are able to prescribe Suboxone to their patients. Additionally, various qualified providers who are able to prescribe medications can write prescriptions for Suboxone, making this medication much more accessible than some other forms of MAT. 2,5,11,15 You can get Suboxone prescribed by a wavered doctor, physician assistant, or nurse practitioner. 2,7,15 

What Substance Addiction Does Health Insurance Cover?

Many major health insurance providers cover treatment for the various substance use disorders, including addictions to alcohol, cocaine, and heroin. Attending rehab for drug addiction can also address when you have an issue with multiple substances.

 

Sources

Sources

Velander, J.R. (2018). Suboxone: Rational, science, misconceptions . The Ochsner Journal, 18(1), 23-29 .

U.S. Department of Health and Human Services. (2020). Does insurance cover medication for opioid addiction? 

CMS.gov. The Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA) .

Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration. (2020). Buprenorphine .

Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration. (2014). Medicaid coverage and financing of medications to treat alcohol and opioid use disorders . HHS Publication No. SMA-14-4854. Rockville, MD: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration. 

U.S. National Library of Medicine. (2020). Opiate and opioid withdrawal . 

National Institute on Drug Abuse. (2018). How effective are medications to treat opioid use disorder? 

Donaher, P.A., & Welsh, C. (2006). Managing opioid addiction with buprenorphine . American Family Physician, 73(9), 1573-1578 .

Center for Substance Abuse Treatment. (2006). Detoxification and substance abuse treatment . Treatment Improvement Protocol (TIP) Series 45, DHHS Publication No. (SMA) 06-4131. Rockville, MD: Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration.

		line-height: 40px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h3 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h4 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}

	p, span, a {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
	}
	p {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 25px;
		margin: 26px 0;
	}
	a {
		color: var(--interactive);
	}


	/* Same styles as p but can be used on <div>s */
	.bold{
		font-weight: bold;
	}
	.text-4xl {
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	.text-3xl {
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
	}
	.text-2xl {
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {
		-moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
		display: inline-block;
		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}
