Medically Reviewed

The Cost to Attend an IOP

 Edited by: Amanda Lautieri, B.A. 

 Reviewed by: Scot Thomas, MD 

Updated Apr 1, 2024

 3 min read · 3 sections 

 
 
 
 Medical Detox 
 Insurance Accepted 
 Same Day Admission 
 Levels of Care 

Professional substance abuse treatment is instrumental in moving someone from a state of addiction and physical dependence towards a place of recovery and wellness. 1 Intensive outpatient programs (IOPs) represent an important level of care on the continuum of treatment options.

As with any treatment for a medical or mental health issue, cost is a major concern for many people, if not the biggest concern. Before considering the cost of an IOP, remember that any fee for addiction treatment is only a general figure and the specific cost will be unique to the facility. The cost for one day of IOP treatment often ranges from $250 to $350 per day. 2 The cost of the program you attend could fall outside this range, however.

 

 
 
 
You can expect an IOP in a relatively less desirable location that offers standard treatment services to a general population to cost less than a program located in a beachside setting that offers a broad range of services from an expert staff. However, because your recovery is so important, when quality treatment is needed, it might not be the best time to focus on cost cutting.

 

Each level of care has its place in recovery. Though it may be tempting to seek the treatment with the lowest cost , it is more important to find the services that meet your needs at the current time. 4 

 

 

What You’re Paying For

Since IOPs are all different, there will be some treatment variation from program to program. As an informed consumer, you’ll want to see where your money is going and if the treatment feels like the right fit for you.

 

 Group therapy is often the primary form of treatment within an IOP, with individual and family sessions also available.

 

Group sessions may take place in a variety of formats and cover different topics.

 

The educational groups may have a larger number of people in each session while the process groups will be smaller and consist of fewer than 10 clients. 5 

 

Now that you know what you are paying for, you may be wondering whether IOPs really work. Fortunately, the answer is yes!

When compared to inpatient rehab, IOPs can be just as beneficial to the recovering person, resulting in outcomes that are comparable to higher levels of treatment. 6 

What If I Can’t Afford an IOP?

Even though IOPs are significantly cheaper than higher levels of care, you may still worry about whether you can afford to attend one. Before you decide it’s too expensive, spend some time exploring your options. You likely have more than you think.

 
 
 
Insurance is the most obvious place to start when it comes to paying for treatment. Often, both private and public/government-funded (Medicare and Medicaid) insurance providers cover numerous mental health and substance abuse services, and as long as there is a medical necessity for IOP, it will likely be covered in some part by your plan.

To avoid a surprise medical bill, call your insurance company directly by using the number on the back of your card to ask them directly how much they’ll cover and which treatment providers are in their network. You can also call specific programs to see if they accept your insurance and how they collect copays.

 

 

A little bit of time spent investigating your options can help you get the treatment you need.

 

Is Standard Outpatient Cheaper?

People who pursue more standard types of outpatient treatment offerings, which could include regularly scheduled individual therapy, group therapy, or a combination of both sessions may only have a time commitment of a few hours a week. People may continue on with standard outpatient therapy for years, as it best suits their recovery needs. 5 This form of outpatient care may include only individual therapy, only group therapy, or a combination of the two. Standard outpatient treatment is usually longer in duration than IOP and can continue for months or years. 5 

As mentioned, the intensity of services is a major factor affecting cost. Because of this, standard outpatient therapy is often cheaper than IOP per week. 

 

Standard outpatient is a fantastic form of care, but it may not be enough to manage all of your symptoms and treatment needs. Clients who receive the enhanced services (e.g., case management) provided in some IOPs have been shown to have better outcomes than those who only received standard outpatient therapy. 5 

A professional evaluation from an addiction expert can help to establish which type of outpatient treatment is best for you at the current time.

 

 Rather than thinking about choosing IOP or standard outpatient, consider IOP and standard outpatient. People who successfully treat a higher level of care often step down to the next lower level to continue their positive trajectory. Standard outpatient is often the step down from IOP.

Try not to let the cost of services discourage you from asking for the help you need to finally achieve the recovery and lifestyle you deserve. At the end of the day, the benefits of a stable life in recovery far outweigh the monetary burden.

	* {
		margin: 0;
		padding: 0;
		box-sizing: border-box;
		-webkit-box-sizing: border-box;
	}
	html, body {
		height: 100%;
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		color: #1C2838;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	html {
		height: 100%;
		width: 100%;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: subpixel-antialiased;
	}

	:root {
		--black: #1a1a1a;
		--white: #ffffff;

		--title-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--copy-font: Inter, -apple-system, BlinkMacSystemFont, Segoe UI, Roboto, Oxygen-Sans, Ubuntu, Cantarell, Helvetica Neue, sans-serif;
		--font-base: 16px;
		--font-base-height: 25px;

		--h1-hero-size: 48px;
		--h1-size: 40px;
		--h2-size: 32px;
		--h3-size: 24px;
		--h4-size: 16px;

		—-breakpoint: 992px;
		—-breakpoint-mobile: 420px;
		—-breakpoint-desktop: 1200px;

		--success: #49a147;
		--warning: #ffad0d;
		--error: #e35141;

		--box-shadow: 0px 5px 20px -5px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);
		--box-shadow-100: 0px 1px 3px 1px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.15),
		0px 1px 2px rgba(0, 0, 0, 0.3);

		--gray-100: #f8f8f8;
		--gray-200: #eeeeee;
		--gray-300: #cccccc;
		--gray-400: #6a6a6a;
		--gray-500: #1a1a1a;

		--primary: #1d2758;
		--primary-100: #e4e5eb;
		--primary-200: #8e93ac;
		--primary-300: #1d2758;
		--primary-400: #151d47;


		--secondary: #eff6f7;
		--secondary-100: #E5EBF5;
		--secondary-200: #92ACD3;
		--secondary-300: #2459A9;
		--secondary-400: #163E8F;

		--tertiary: #2459a9;
		--tertiary-100: #FAFCFD;
		--tertiary-200: #F4F9F9;
		--tertiary-300: #EFF6F7;
		--tertiary-400: #E4EFF1;

		--interactive: #c77124;
		--interactive-100: #f8eee5;
		--interactive-200: #e3b892;
		--interactive-300: #c77124;
		--interactive-400: #ba5e1b;

		--accent-200: #F4F9F9;

		--focus: #005FCC;

		--gradient-100: linear-gradient(183.11deg, rgba(248, 238, 229, 0.4) 2.57%, rgba(229, 235, 245, 0.4) 97.42%);
		--gradient-200: linear-gradient(90deg, #EFF6F7 0.02%, #F8EEE5 99.96%);
	}

	h1 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	h2 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h3 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
		margin: 30px 0 24px;
	}
	h4 {
		font-family: var(--title-font);
		font-weight: bold;
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}

	p, span, a {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
	}
	p {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 25px;
		margin: 26px 0;
	}
	a {
		color: var(--interactive);
	}


	/* Same styles as p but can be used on <div>s */
	.bold{
		font-weight: bold;
	}
	.text-4xl {
		font-size: 36px;
		line-height: 44px;
	}
	.text-3xl {
		font-size: 30px;
		line-height: 40px;
	}
	.text-2xl {
		font-size: 24px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-xl {
		font-size: 20px;
		line-height: 32px;
	}
	.text-lg {
		font-size: 18px;
		line-height: 28px;
	}
	.text-base {
		font-size: 16px;
		line-height: 24px;
	}
	.text-sm {
		font-size: 14px;
		line-height: 20px;
	}
	.text-xs {
		font-size: 12px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}
	.text-xxs {
		font-size: 10px;
		line-height: 16px;
	}









	button a {
		text-decoration: none;
		color: inherit;
	}
	blockquote {
		font-size: 23px;
		letter-spacing: -0.3px;
		line-height: 34px;
	}
	button {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 12px;
		letter-spacing: 0;
		line-height: 15px;
		text-align: center;
		padding: 15px;
	}
	input, textarea {
		padding: 5px 10px;
		border: 1px solid var(--gray-300);
		display: block;
		font-size: 16px;
	}
	label {
		font-family: var(--copy-font);
		font-size: 14px;
		display: inline-block;
		padding-bottom: 5px;
	}
	iframe {
		max-width: 100%;
	}
	img {
		vertical-align: middle;
		max-width: 100%;
		height: auto;
	}
	ul, ol {
		padding: 0 0 0 20px;
		margin: 10px 0;
		line-height: 1.6;
	}

	ol li {
		padding-left: 10px;
	}

	hr {
		margin: 50px 0;
		border: 1px solid #f0f0f0;
	}

	.error-message {
		color: var(--error);
		padding-top: 10px;
		font-size: 13px;
		font-weight: bold;
	}

	/* Make clicks pass-through */
	#nprogress {
		pointer-events: none;
	}

	#nprogress .bar {
		background: #337fbd;

		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 0;
		left: 0;

		width: 100%;
		height: 2px;
	}

	/* Fancy blur effect */
	#nprogress .peg {
		display: block;
		position: absolute;
		right: 0px;
		width: 100px;
		height: 100%;
		box-shadow: 0 0 10px #337fbd, 0 0 5px #337fbd;
		opacity: 1;

		-webkit-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		-ms-transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
		transform: rotate(3deg) translate(0px, -4px);
	}

	/* Remove these to get rid of the spinner */
	#nprogress .spinner {
		display: block;
		position: fixed;
		z-index: 1031;
		top: 15px;
		right: 15px;
	}

	#nprogress .spinner-icon {
		width: 18px;
		height: 18px;
		box-sizing: border-box;

		border: solid 2px transparent;
		border-top-color: #29d;
		border-left-color: #29d;
		border-radius: 50%;

		-webkit-animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
		animation: nprogress-spinner 400ms linear infinite;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent {
		overflow: hidden;
		position: relative;
	}

	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .spinner,
	.nprogress-custom-parent #nprogress .bar {
		position: absolute;
	}
	.bcFloat,
	.widget-floating__teaser {
		display: none !important;
	}
	@-webkit-keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			-webkit-transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}
	@keyframes nprogress-spinner {
		0% {
			transform: rotate(0deg);
		}
		100% {
			transform: rotate(360deg);
		}
	}

	/* font_awesome styles */
	.fa,.fab,.fal,.far,.fas {
		-moz-osx-font-smoothing: grayscale;
		-webkit-font-smoothing: antialiased;
		display: inline-block;
		font-style: normal;
		font-variant: normal;
		text-rendering: auto;
		line-height: 1
	}

offer care similar to that of inpatient programs, just at a lower intensity. Plus, patients live at home, often allowing them to maintain some family and work responsibilities while receiving treatment. Learn about Rhode Island’s IOPs, including length of stay, levels of care, and payment options. </div><div class="jsx-4001556473 hero__highlights"><div class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f container"><div style="margin:0 0 15px 0" class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f text-sm bold">What you will learn:</div><div class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f item"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="transform:rotate(0deg)"><path d="M20.8389 6.69461L8.81811 18.7154L3.16125 13.0586L4.57125 11.6486L8.81811 15.8854L19.4289 5.28461L20.8389 6.69461Z" fill="var(--success)"></path></svg><div style="margin-left:15px" class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f">IOP insights.</div></div><div class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f item"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="transform:rotate(0deg)"><path d="M20.8389 6.69461L8.81811 18.7154L3.16125 13.0586L4.57125 11.6486L8.81811 15.8854L19.4289 5.28461L20.8389 6.69461Z" fill="var(--success)"></path></svg><div style="margin-left:15px" class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f">How to locate an IOP in Rhode Island.</div></div><div class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f item"><svg width="24" height="24" viewBox="0 0 24 24" fill="none" style="transform:rotate(0deg)"><path d="M20.8389 6.69461L8.81811 18.7154L3.16125 13.0586L4.57125 11.6486L8.81811 15.8854L19.4289 5.28461L20.8389 6.69461Z" fill="var(--success)"></path></svg><div style="margin-left:15px" class="jsx-acda82bbe253fc7f">Insurance and other payment options.</div></div></div></div></div></div><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-726436161 container"><div class="jsx-123217fb97058cc4 jsx-726436161 content"><div id="wordpress-content" class="jsx-1559800555"><div class="jsx-1559800555"><div class="jsx-1559800555 toc__container"><div class="sc-ae1ffb5e-0 emMzuR"><div></div></div></div><div class="jsx-1559800555"><div><h2><strong>What is an Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP)?</strong></h2>
<p>An <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/intensive-outpatient-programs/cost" target="_blank" rel="noopener">intensive outpatient program</a> is a structured form of <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/outpatient-treatment" target="_blank" rel="noopener">outpatient treatment</a>, which means patients live at home while attending treatment in settings such as community mental health centers, clinics, counselors’ offices, hospitals, and outpatient offices within residential programs.<sup>1</sup></p>
<p>The <a href="https://www.asam.org/asam-criteria/about-the-asam-criteria" target="_blank" rel="noopener">American Society of Addiction Medicine</a> has outlined the various <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/levels-of-care" target="_blank" rel="noopener">levels of care</a> available for drug and alcohol treatment using a scale of .5 to 4. According to the ASAM criteria, IOPs comprise level 2.1. As such, they offer a higher level of care than traditional outpatient programs and a lower level of care than <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/partial-hospitalization-programs" target="_blank" rel="noopener">partial hospitalization programs (PHPs)</a> and <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/inpatient" target="_blank" rel="noopener">inpatient services</a>.<sup>2</sup> Thus, IOPs provide similar care to that of inpatient programs, such as behavioral therapies, peer support, and psychoeducation, just at a lower intensity.<sup>3</sup></p>
<p>While the intensity levels and participation requirements vary among IOPs, many IOPs in Rhode Island require 6 to 30 hours per week of treatment and last for 90 days.<sup>3,4</sup> Once someone completes an IOP, they often transition into traditional outpatient care and/or aftercare.<sup>3</sup></p>
<p>Treatment isn’t a one-size-fits-all solution, so it’s important to work with a professional to match your needs to the appropriate level of care. However, professionals often recommend IOPs for those with mild to moderate substance misuse issues who don’t require (or have already completed) detox and who don’t need 24/7 care.<sup>4</sup> Thus, IOPs are often helpful for those who need some form of ongoing, structured treatment and accountability but also have home or work responsibilities to maintain.</p>
<p>Additionally, those best suited to an IOP will likely have the continued desire to complete the program as well as reliable transportation to and from sessions, sober living conditions, and a supportive home environment.<sup>1,5</sup></p>
<h2><strong>Where Can You Find an IOP in Rhode Island?</strong></h2>
<p>You can find IOPs throughout Rhode Island, but many are located near metropolitan areas such as Providence, Warwick, Cranston, Pawtucket, and Woonsocket. All told, Rhode Island is home to 54 substance use treatment centers, of which 17 provide IOPs.</p>
<p>If visiting a physical location is inconvenient, some IOPs offer at least some services via <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/telehealth" target="_blank" rel="noopener">telehealth</a>. That is, you may be able to participate in various therapies with counselors, healthcare providers, peers, etc. via phone and online. According to <a href="https://www.FindTreatment.gov" target="_blank" rel="noopener">FindTreatment.gov</a>, which is offered by the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA), 40 treatment facilities in Rhode Island offer telehealth rehab services, and all 17 of the state’s IOP providers indicate that they offer telehealth options.<sup>6</sup></p>
<p>Several IOP options in Rhode Island are available through American Addiction Centers. Its AdCare Detox Rhode Island facilities—located in Warwick, Greenville, and South Kingstown—offer a host of outpatient options, including IOP services. Additionally, the nearby <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/treatment-centers/adcare-rhode-island" target="_blank" rel="noopener">AdCare</a> in North Kingstown, RI, and <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/treatment-centers/adcare-hospital" target="_blank" rel="noopener">AdCare Treatment Hospital</a> in Worcester, MA, include inpatient programs as well as some forms of outpatient care.</p>
<h2><strong> What Substances Do IOPs in Rhode Island Treat?</strong></h2>
<p>Most IOPs in Rhode Island treat various drug and alcohol addictions, which means help is available regardless of your substance of choice. IOPs typically treat disorders related to the following substances and more:</p>
<ul>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/alcohol" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Alcohol</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/benzodiazepine" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Benzodiazepines</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/stimulants/cocaine" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Cocaine</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/opioids/fentanyl" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Fentanyl</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/hallucinogens" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Hallucinogens</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/opioids/heroin/rehab-treatment" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Heroin</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/opioids" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Opioids</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/stimulants/meth/rehab-treatment" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Methamphetamine</a>.</li>
<li><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/prescription-drugs" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Prescription drugs</a>.</li>
</ul>
<p>Additionally, some facilities cater to those who participate in polysubstance use. This is when someone intentionally or unintentionally uses more than one substance within a short period of time. A prime example is when <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/alcohol/mixing-opioids" target="_blank" rel="noopener">alcohol is mixed with opioids</a>, which can be extremely dangerous. And along with drug and alcohol care, 11 of the 17 IOPs in Rhode Island offer treatment for co-occurring disorders and polysubstance use, including <a href="/treatment-centers" target="_blank" rel="noopener">AAC’s rehabs</a>.</p>
<p>Also note that many outpatient rehab programs in Rhode Island treat both substance use disorders as well as <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/co-occurring-disorders" target="_blank" rel="noopener">co-occurring mental conditions</a>, such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and personality disorders.<sup>6,7 </sup>Research suggests that treating both disorders concurrently is more effective at promoting long-term recovery than treating them independently.<sup>5</sup></p>
<h2><strong>How Much Does an Intensive Outpatient Program in Rhode Island Cost?</strong></h2>
<p>The cost of an IOP in Rhode Island can vary widely depending on several factors, such as the facility you choose, the services you need, the length of care, and how you pay for treatment. Each treatment center varies in price, and those with upscale amenities or specialized treatment will typically cost more.</p>
<p>If you have insurance, plan variables (including deductibles, co-pays, etc.) can affect how much you pay out-of-pocket for treatment. To figure out just how much you’ll pay for treatment with your insurance plan, reach out to AAC at <span class="portal_phone_link" id="portal_phone_link-69a80864019fd"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"helpline":"true"},"children":""}</script></span>, and an admissions navigator can assist you with this process.</p>
<p>If you don’t have insurance, don’t worry. There are other ways to pay for IOPs as described below.</p>
<h2><strong>Does Insurance Cover IOPs in Rhode Island?</strong></h2>
<p>Although levels of coverage vary, two key regulations ensure you receive assistance for substance use disorder treatment. The Affordable Care Act (ACA) requires that all health insurance companies provide addiction treatment. That means at least a portion of the costs for all essential health benefits, including mental health and addiction care, are covered under the ACA.<sup>8</sup></p>
<p>The <a href="https://www.cms.gov/cciio/programs-and-initiatives/other-insurance-protections/mhpaea_factsheet" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA)</a> also ensures you’ll receive benefits for mental health and addiction treatment services that are on par with other benefits, such as medical or surgical care. Under this act, health insurance companies can’t impose unfavorable benefit limitations on mental health and addiction care.<sup>9 </sup></p>
<p>Beyond private health insurance, other insurance programs such as <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/medicare" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Medicare</a>, HealthSource RI (i.e., the state’s version of <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/using-medicaid-to-pay-for-rehab" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Medicaid</a>), and <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/tricare" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Tricare</a> (for military personnel, veterans, and their families) fully or partially cover IOP treatment. Currently, 10 IOPs in Rhode Island accept Medicare, 16 accept Medicaid, and 10 accept Tricare.<sup>6 </sup>Like private insurance, your level of coverage differs based on your plan.</p>
<p>Even if you don’t have insurance, there several other ways to pay for treatment, including:</p>
<ul>
<li><strong>Grants and scholarships</strong>. Many community, state, and government programs provide financial assistance for people seeking SUD treatment. For example, the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) offers <a href="https://www.samhsa.gov/grants/block-grants" target="_blank" rel="noopener">block grants</a> for those struggling with addiction. Five Rhode Island IOPs indicate that they accept SAMHSA Block Grant funding.<sup>10</sup></li>
<li><strong>Payment plans and sliding fee scales</strong>. Some treatment centers provide flexible financing options and/or sliding fee scales, where the amount you pay is adjusted based on your income. For example, data on FindTreatment.gov indicates that 2 IOPs in the state offer payment plans and/or sliding fee scales.<sup>6</sup></li>
<li><strong>Loans and credit</strong>. Private loans from companies specializing in addiction-treatment lending, a bank, or a loved one may also be available. Another option is health and wellness financing credit cards, such as CareCredit. Often, these healthcare financing options charge no interest for 6 to 24 months. However, you may need to pay off the entire balance within the allotted time period or risk being hit with significant interest charges.</li>
<li><strong>Indian Health Service (IHS)/Tribal/Urban—ITU Funds. </strong>Two IOPs in Rhode Island accept ITU funds, which are available through the Indian healthcare system.<sup>6</sup></li>
</ul>
<p>Also keep in mind that <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/free" target="_blank" rel="noopener">free rehab</a> and/or <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/state-funded" target="_blank" rel="noopener">state-funded programs,</a> as well as programs and funding for special populations such as women, adolescents, Veterans, members of the LGBTQ+ community, etc., may be available for those who qualify.</p>
<p><a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/treatment-centers/rhode-island" target="_blank" rel="noopener">Rhode Island offers myriad treatment options</a>, including IOPs, to suit each individual’s unique needs. With facilities scattered throughout the country, including some in Rhode Island and nearby Massachusetts, <a href="https://americanaddictioncenters.org/" target="_blank" rel="noopener">American Addiction Centers</a> offers services at all levels of care. Contact AAC at <span class="portal_phone_link" id="portal_phone_link-69a8086401a0c"><script crossorigin type="application/json">{"props":{"helpline":"true"},"children":""}</script></span> to learn more about IOPs in Rhode Island, explore payment options, and take the first steps toward recovery today.</p>

</script><script id="__NEXT_DATA__" type="application/json">{"props":{"pageProps":{"_sentryTraceData":"9c36c9076e8d409f8531665fc4fc6850-9eb1aa481141e986-0","_sentryBaggage":"sentry-environment=production,sentry-release=odsUSnXHa7Xks8o0SB39f,sentry-public_key=96a2ce264cf34a8eb5bbd664e8a614e8,sentry-trace_id=9c36c9076e8d409f8531665fc4fc6850,sentry-sample_rate=0.01,sentry-transaction=%2F%5B...pathname%5D,sentry-sampled=false","data":{"id":102399,"date":"2023-06-28T16:07:57","modified":"2024-08-23T11:51:36","slug":"rhode-island","type":"page","link":"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/intensive-outpatient-programs/rhode-island","title":{"rendered":"Intensive Outpatient Programs in Rhode Island: Finding IOP in RI"},"content":{"rendered":"\u003cdiv\u003e\u003ch2\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eWhat is an Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP)?\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eAn \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/intensive-outpatient-programs/cost\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eintensive outpatient program\u003c/a\u003e is a structured form of \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/outpatient-treatment\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eoutpatient treatment\u003c/a\u003e, which means patients live at home while attending treatment in settings such as community mental health centers, clinics, counselors’ offices, hospitals, and outpatient offices within residential programs.\u003csup\u003e1\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe \u003ca href=\"https://www.asam.org/asam-criteria/about-the-asam-criteria\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eAmerican Society of Addiction Medicine\u003c/a\u003e has outlined the various \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/levels-of-care\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003elevels of care\u003c/a\u003e available for drug and alcohol treatment using a scale of .5 to 4. According to the ASAM criteria, IOPs comprise level 2.1. As such, they offer a higher level of care than traditional outpatient programs and a lower level of care than \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/partial-hospitalization-programs\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003epartial hospitalization programs (PHPs)\u003c/a\u003e and \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/inpatient\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003einpatient services\u003c/a\u003e.\u003csup\u003e2\u003c/sup\u003e Thus, IOPs provide similar care to that of inpatient programs, such as behavioral therapies, peer support, and psychoeducation, just at a lower intensity.\u003csup\u003e3\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eWhile the intensity levels and participation requirements vary among IOPs, many IOPs in Rhode Island require 6 to 30 hours per week of treatment and last for 90 days.\u003csup\u003e3,4\u003c/sup\u003e Once someone completes an IOP, they often transition into traditional outpatient care and/or aftercare.\u003csup\u003e3\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eTreatment isn’t a one-size-fits-all solution, so it’s important to work with a professional to match your needs to the appropriate level of care. However, professionals often recommend IOPs for those with mild to moderate substance misuse issues who don’t require (or have already completed) detox and who don’t need 24/7 care.\u003csup\u003e4\u003c/sup\u003e Thus, IOPs are often helpful for those who need some form of ongoing, structured treatment and accountability but also have home or work responsibilities to maintain.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eAdditionally, those best suited to an IOP will likely have the continued desire to complete the program as well as reliable transportation to and from sessions, sober living conditions, and a supportive home environment.\u003csup\u003e1,5\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eWhere Can You Find an IOP in Rhode Island?\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eYou can find IOPs throughout Rhode Island, but many are located near metropolitan areas such as Providence, Warwick, Cranston, Pawtucket, and Woonsocket. All told, Rhode Island is home to 54 substance use treatment centers, of which 17 provide IOPs.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIf visiting a physical location is inconvenient, some IOPs offer at least some services via \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/telehealth\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003etelehealth\u003c/a\u003e. That is, you may be able to participate in various therapies with counselors, healthcare providers, peers, etc. via phone and online. According to \u003ca href=\"http://www.FindTreatment.gov\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eFindTreatment.gov\u003c/a\u003e, which is offered by the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA), 40 treatment facilities in Rhode Island offer telehealth rehab services, and all 17 of the state’s IOP providers indicate that they offer telehealth options.\u003csup\u003e6\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eSeveral IOP options in Rhode Island are available through American Addiction Centers. Its AdCare Detox Rhode Island facilities—located in Warwick, Greenville, and South Kingstown—offer a host of outpatient options, including IOP services. Additionally, the nearby \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/treatment-centers/adcare-rhode-island\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eAdCare\u003c/a\u003e in North Kingstown, RI, and \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/treatment-centers/adcare-hospital\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eAdCare Treatment Hospital\u003c/a\u003e in Worcester, MA, include inpatient programs as well as some forms of outpatient care.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003e\u003cstrong\u003e What Substances Do IOPs in Rhode Island Treat?\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eMost IOPs in Rhode Island treat various drug and alcohol addictions, which means help is available regardless of your substance of choice. IOPs typically treat disorders related to the following substances and more:\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/alcohol\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eAlcohol\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/benzodiazepine\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eBenzodiazepines\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/stimulants/cocaine\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eCocaine\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/opioids/fentanyl\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eFentanyl\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/hallucinogens\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eHallucinogens\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/opioids/heroin/rehab-treatment\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eHeroin\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/opioids\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eOpioids\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/stimulants/meth/rehab-treatment\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eMethamphetamine\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/prescription-drugs\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003ePrescription drugs\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eAdditionally, some facilities cater to those who participate in polysubstance use. This is when someone intentionally or unintentionally uses more than one substance within a short period of time. A prime example is when \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/alcohol/mixing-opioids\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003ealcohol is mixed with opioids\u003c/a\u003e, which can be extremely dangerous. And along with drug and alcohol care, 11 of the 17 IOPs in Rhode Island offer treatment for co-occurring disorders and polysubstance use, including \u003ca href=\"/treatment-centers\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eAAC’s rehabs\u003c/a\u003e.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eAlso note that many outpatient rehab programs in Rhode Island treat both substance use disorders as well as \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/co-occurring-disorders\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eco-occurring mental conditions\u003c/a\u003e, such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and personality disorders.\u003csup\u003e6,7 \u003c/sup\u003eResearch suggests that treating both disorders concurrently is more effective at promoting long-term recovery than treating them independently.\u003csup\u003e5\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eHow Much Does an Intensive Outpatient Program in Rhode Island Cost?\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe cost of an IOP in Rhode Island can vary widely depending on several factors, such as the facility you choose, the services you need, the length of care, and how you pay for treatment. Each treatment center varies in price, and those with upscale amenities or specialized treatment will typically cost more.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIf you have insurance, plan variables (including deductibles, co-pays, etc.) can affect how much you pay out-of-pocket for treatment. To figure out just how much you’ll pay for treatment with your insurance plan, reach out to AAC at \u003cspan class=\"portal_phone_link\" id=\"portal_phone_link-69a80864019fd\"\u003e\u003cscript crossorigin type=\"application/json\"\u003e{\"props\":{\"helpline\":\"true\"},\"children\":\"\"}\u003c/script\u003e\u003c/span\u003e, and an admissions navigator can assist you with this process.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eIf you don’t have insurance, don’t worry. There are other ways to pay for IOPs as described below.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003ch2\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eDoes Insurance Cover IOPs in Rhode Island?\u003c/strong\u003e\u003c/h2\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eAlthough levels of coverage vary, two key regulations ensure you receive assistance for substance use disorder treatment. The Affordable Care Act (ACA) requires that all health insurance companies provide addiction treatment. That means at least a portion of the costs for all essential health benefits, including mental health and addiction care, are covered under the ACA.\u003csup\u003e8\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eThe \u003ca href=\"https://www.cms.gov/cciio/programs-and-initiatives/other-insurance-protections/mhpaea_factsheet\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eMental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA)\u003c/a\u003e also ensures you’ll receive benefits for mental health and addiction treatment services that are on par with other benefits, such as medical or surgical care. Under this act, health insurance companies can’t impose unfavorable benefit limitations on mental health and addiction care.\u003csup\u003e9 \u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eBeyond private health insurance, other insurance programs such as \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/medicare\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eMedicare\u003c/a\u003e, HealthSource RI (i.e., the state’s version of \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/using-medicaid-to-pay-for-rehab\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eMedicaid\u003c/a\u003e), and \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/insurance-coverage/tricare\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eTricare\u003c/a\u003e (for military personnel, veterans, and their families) fully or partially cover IOP treatment. Currently, 10 IOPs in Rhode Island accept Medicare, 16 accept Medicaid, and 10 accept Tricare.\u003csup\u003e6 \u003c/sup\u003eLike private insurance, your level of coverage differs based on your plan.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eEven if you don’t have insurance, there several other ways to pay for treatment, including:\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cul\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eGrants and scholarships\u003c/strong\u003e. Many community, state, and government programs provide financial assistance for people seeking SUD treatment. For example, the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) offers \u003ca href=\"https://www.samhsa.gov/grants/block-grants\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eblock grants\u003c/a\u003e for those struggling with addiction. Five Rhode Island IOPs indicate that they accept SAMHSA Block Grant funding.\u003csup\u003e10\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003ePayment plans and sliding fee scales\u003c/strong\u003e. Some treatment centers provide flexible financing options and/or sliding fee scales, where the amount you pay is adjusted based on your income. For example, data on FindTreatment.gov indicates that 2 IOPs in the state offer payment plans and/or sliding fee scales.\u003csup\u003e6\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eLoans and credit\u003c/strong\u003e. Private loans from companies specializing in addiction-treatment lending, a bank, or a loved one may also be available. Another option is health and wellness financing credit cards, such as CareCredit. Often, these healthcare financing options charge no interest for 6 to 24 months. However, you may need to pay off the entire balance within the allotted time period or risk being hit with significant interest charges.\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003cli\u003e\u003cstrong\u003eIndian Health Service (IHS)/Tribal/Urban—ITU Funds. \u003c/strong\u003eTwo IOPs in Rhode Island accept ITU funds, which are available through the Indian healthcare system.\u003csup\u003e6\u003c/sup\u003e\u003c/li\u003e\n\u003c/ul\u003e\n\u003cp\u003eAlso keep in mind that \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/free\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003efree rehab\u003c/a\u003e and/or \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/rehab-guide/state-funded\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003estate-funded programs,\u003c/a\u003e as well as programs and funding for special populations such as women, adolescents, Veterans, members of the LGBTQ+ community, etc., may be available for those who qualify.\u003c/p\u003e\n\u003cp\u003e\u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/treatment-centers/rhode-island\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eRhode Island offers myriad treatment options\u003c/a\u003e, including IOPs, to suit each individual’s unique needs. With facilities scattered throughout the country, including some in Rhode Island and nearby Massachusetts, \u003ca href=\"https://americanaddictioncenters.org/\" target=\"_blank\" rel=\"noopener\"\u003eAmerican Addiction Centers\u003c/a\u003e offers services at all levels of care. Intensive Outpatient Programs in Rhode Island: Finding IOP in RI

 Written by: Mariah Bourne 

 Edited by: Linda Armstrong 

Updated Aug 23, 2024

 3 min read · 5 sections 

 
 
 
 Medical Detox 
 Insurance Accepted 
 Same Day Admission 

As a type of outpatient treatment for drug and alcohol misuse, intensive outpatient programs (IOPs) in Rhode Island offer care similar to that of inpatient programs, just at a lower intensity. Plus, patients live at home, often allowing them to maintain some family and work responsibilities while receiving treatment. Learn about Rhode Island’s IOPs, including length of stay, levels of care, and payment options. 

What you will learn:

 
 
IOP insights.

 
 
How to locate an IOP in Rhode Island.

 
 
Insurance and other payment options.

 What is an Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP)? 

An intensive outpatient program is a structured form of outpatient treatment , which means patients live at home while attending treatment in settings such as community mental health centers, clinics, counselors’ offices, hospitals, and outpatient offices within residential programs. 1 

The American Society of Addiction Medicine has outlined the various levels of care available for drug and alcohol treatment using a scale of .5 to 4. According to the ASAM criteria, IOPs comprise level 2.1. As such, they offer a higher level of care than traditional outpatient programs and a lower level of care than partial hospitalization programs (PHPs) and inpatient services . 2 Thus, IOPs provide similar care to that of inpatient programs, such as behavioral therapies, peer support, and psychoeducation, just at a lower intensity. 3 

While the intensity levels and participation requirements vary among IOPs, many IOPs in Rhode Island require 6 to 30 hours per week of treatment and last for 90 days. 3,4 Once someone completes an IOP, they often transition into traditional outpatient care and/or aftercare. 3 

Treatment isn’t a one-size-fits-all solution, so it’s important to work with a professional to match your needs to the appropriate level of care. However, professionals often recommend IOPs for those with mild to moderate substance misuse issues who don’t require (or have already completed) detox and who don’t need 24/7 care. 4 Thus, IOPs are often helpful for those who need some form of ongoing, structured treatment and accountability but also have home or work responsibilities to maintain.

Additionally, those best suited to an IOP will likely have the continued desire to complete the program as well as reliable transportation to and from sessions, sober living conditions, and a supportive home environment. 1,5 

 Where Can You Find an IOP in Rhode Island? 

You can find IOPs throughout Rhode Island, but many are located near metropolitan areas such as Providence, Warwick, Cranston, Pawtucket, and Woonsocket. All told, Rhode Island is home to 54 substance use treatment centers, of which 17 provide IOPs.

If visiting a physical location is inconvenient, some IOPs offer at least some services via telehealth . That is, you may be able to participate in various therapies with counselors, healthcare providers, peers, etc. via phone and online. According to FindTreatment.gov , which is offered by the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA), 40 treatment facilities in Rhode Island offer telehealth rehab services, and all 17 of the state’s IOP providers indicate that they offer telehealth options. 6 

Several IOP options in Rhode Island are available through American Addiction Centers. Its AdCare Detox Rhode Island facilities—located in Warwick, Greenville, and South Kingstown—offer a host of outpatient options, including IOP services. Additionally, the nearby AdCare in North Kingstown, RI, and AdCare Treatment Hospital in Worcester, MA, include inpatient programs as well as some forms of outpatient care.

 What Substances Do IOPs in Rhode Island Treat? 

Most IOPs in Rhode Island treat various drug and alcohol addictions, which means help is available regardless of your substance of choice. IOPs typically treat disorders related to the following substances and more:

 

 Alcohol .

 Benzodiazepines .

 Cocaine .

 Fentanyl .

 Hallucinogens .

 Heroin .

 Opioids .

 Methamphetamine .

 Prescription drugs .

 

Additionally, some facilities cater to those who participate in polysubstance use. This is when someone intentionally or unintentionally uses more than one substance within a short period of time. A prime example is when alcohol is mixed with opioids , which can be extremely dangerous. And along with drug and alcohol care, 11 of the 17 IOPs in Rhode Island offer treatment for co-occurring disorders and polysubstance use, including AAC’s rehabs .

Also note that many outpatient rehab programs in Rhode Island treat both substance use disorders as well as co-occurring mental conditions , such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and personality disorders. 6,7 Research suggests that treating both disorders concurrently is more effective at promoting long-term recovery than treating them independently. 5 

 How Much Does an Intensive Outpatient Program in Rhode Island Cost? 

The cost of an IOP in Rhode Island can vary widely depending on several factors, such as the facility you choose, the services you need, the length of care, and how you pay for treatment. Each treatment center varies in price, and those with upscale amenities or specialized treatment will typically cost more.

If you have insurance, plan variables (including deductibles, co-pays, etc.) can affect how much you pay out-of-pocket for treatment. To figure out just how much you’ll pay for treatment with your insurance plan, reach out to AAC at , and an admissions navigator can assist you with this process.

If you don’t have insurance, don’t worry. There are other ways to pay for IOPs as described below.

 Does Insurance Cover IOPs in Rhode Island? 

Although levels of coverage vary, two key regulations ensure you receive assistance for substance use disorder treatment. The Affordable Care Act (ACA) requires that all health insurance companies provide addiction treatment. That means at least a portion of the costs for all essential health benefits, including mental health and addiction care, are covered under the ACA. 8 

The Mental Health Parity and Addiction Equity Act (MHPAEA) also ensures you’ll receive benefits for mental health and addiction treatment services that are on par with other benefits, such as medical or surgical care. Under this act, health insurance companies can’t impose unfavorable benefit limitations on mental health and addiction care. 9  

Beyond private health insurance, other insurance programs such as Medicare , HealthSource RI (i.e., the state’s version of Medicaid ), and Tricare (for military personnel, veterans, and their families) fully or partially cover IOP treatment. Currently, 10 IOPs in Rhode Island accept Medicare, 16 accept Medicaid, and 10 accept Tricare. 6 Like private insurance, your level of coverage differs based on your plan.

Even if you don’t have insurance, there several other ways to pay for treatment, including:

 

 Grants and scholarships . Many community, state, and government programs provide financial assistance for people seeking SUD treatment. For example, the Substance Abuse and Mental Health Services Administration (SAMHSA) offers block grants for those struggling with addiction. Five Rhode Island IOPs indicate that they accept SAMHSA Block Grant funding. 10 

 Payment plans and sliding fee scales . Some treatment centers provide flexible financing options and/or sliding fee scales, where the amount you pay is adjusted based on your income. For example, data on FindTreatment.gov indicates that 2 IOPs in the state offer payment plans and/or sliding fee scales. 6 

 Loans and credit . Private loans from companies specializing in addiction-treatment lending, a bank, or a loved one may also be available. Another option is health and wellness financing credit cards, such as CareCredit. Often, these healthcare financing options charge no interest for 6 to 24 months. However, you may need to pay off the entire balance within the allotted time period or risk being hit with significant interest charges.

 Indian Health Service (IHS)/Tribal/Urban—ITU Funds. Two IOPs in Rhode Island accept ITU funds, which are available through the Indian healthcare system. 6 

 

Also keep in mind that free rehab and/or state-funded programs, as well as programs and funding for special populations such as women, adolescents, Veterans, members of the LGBTQ+ community, etc., may be available for those who qualify.

 Rhode Island offers myriad treatment options , including IOPs, to suit each individual’s unique needs. With facilities scattered throughout the country, including some in Rhode Island and nearby Massachusetts, American Addiction Centers offers services at all levels of care. Contact AAC at to learn more about IOPs in Rhode Island, explore payment options, and take the first steps toward recovery today.

rawMentions: Medicaid .
Does Medicaid Cover Suboxone?
Medicaid generally does cover the Suboxone cost for treatment of opioid use disorder, but the extent of coverage depends on various factors.
Suboxone is listed on the preferred drug list for Medicaid in all but one state, although the majority of these states require prior authorization.

Prior authorization means that the medical professional prescribing Suboxone has to obtain approval from Medicaid before the medication will be approved, and may have to submit documentation showing that it is medically necessary, or that the person is receiving counseling in addition to medication.
Medicaid imposes limits on how long a person can stay on Suboxone, although the limit depends on your state and some states set lifetime limits.
If you exceed this lifetime limit, coverage for Suboxone may be denied through Medicaid.

rawMentions: Residential Treatment
Inpatient Rehab
Suboxone costs can be different depending on the provider that you obtain your prescription from, whether it is through a doctor’s office, an inpatient facility, or an outpatient clinic.
5 American Addiction Centers has a full range of treatment programs, from detox to inpatient care to outpatient care, providing the highest quality treatment across the country.
Inpatient or residential rehab.
While the therapies offered are similar to those of inpatient programs, IOP care is slightly lower in intensity.
While IOPs provide therapies similar to those offered via inpatient facilities, care is slightly less intense and less frequent, and patients stay in their own homes as opposed to the facility.
Other times, however, IOPs are used to help those formerly in inpatient settings transition to a lower level of care, allowing them to ease into a new normal following intensive treatment.
However, therapies at Florida IOPs are similar to inpatient facilities; they’re just provided at a slightly lower intensity.
Provided in clinics, rehab facilities, counseling offices, and more, IOPs in Florida offer therapies similar to that of inpatient facilities.
For others, IOPs are a step down from inpatient programs , as they help patients to transition successfully from inpatient rehab into the new normal of everyday life.
4 Compared to inpatient programs where patients receive care 24/7 and are surrounded by supportive peers and professionals, an unsupportive home environment can be problematic for recovery.
Intensive outpatient programs (IOPs) in Nevada offer similar care to inpatient facilities but at a lower intensity.
IOPs provide a higher level of care than traditional outpatient treatment , but they’re a step down from inpatient programs where patients stay in a rehab facility 24/7.
1,2 That said, IOPs provide similar care and therapies to those found in inpatient settings, just at a lower intensity.
Intensive outpatient programs can help people transition out of inpatient treatment and into their new normal.
5 In an inpatient program, patients receive care and attention 24/7, and they’re surrounded by counselors, medical professionals, and peers.

Whether you’re looking for an intensive outpatient program in Las Vegas, Nevada or another form of treatment, AAC admissions navigators at can not only help you explore AAC’s treatment centers but also verify your insurance and answer your questions about detox, inpatient, outpatient, sober living, telehealth services, and more.
PHP care is lower in intensity than inpatient and residential treatment—but higher than:
One step down from the intensity of residential care
Services mirror residential programs with the added flexibility of living at home
PHPs are a cost-effective treatment option compared to inpatient or residential treatment programs.

Partial hospitalization programs (PHP)—aka day treatment—are an intensive and highly structured form of outpatient rehab that allows patients to live at home as opposed to within an inpatient treatment center .
This middle-ground intensity typically comes at a somewhat lower cost than inpatient care, while still offering access to clinical therapies and medical oversight.
Partial hospitalization programs are often employed following inpatient treatment as a form of continuing care or as a first step in recovery, possibly immediately after detox.
Representing the midway point between inpatient and traditional outpatient treatment, PHPs offer many of the same therapeutic interventions found in inpatient treatment.
Many of the therapies and services provided in inpatient care are also available in partial hospitalization programs.
When compared to inpatient rehab, IOPs can be just as beneficial to the recovering person, resulting in outcomes that are comparable to higher levels of treatment.

An intensive outpatient program is a structured form of outpatient treatment , which means patients live at home while attending treatment in settings such as community mental health centers, clinics, counselors’ offices, hospitals, and outpatient offices within residential programs.
As a type of outpatient treatment for drug and alcohol misuse, intensive outpatient programs (IOPs) in Rhode Island offer care similar to that of inpatient programs, just at a lower intensity.
As such, they offer a higher level of care than traditional outpatient programs and a lower level of care than partial hospitalization programs (PHPs) and inpatient services .
2 Thus, IOPs provide similar care to that of inpatient programs, such as behavioral therapies, peer support, and psychoeducation, just at a lower intensity.
Additionally, the nearby AdCare in North Kingstown, RI, and AdCare Treatment Hospital in Worcester, MA, include inpatient programs as well as some forms of outpatient care.

Representing the midway point between inpatient and traditional outpatient treatment, PHPs offer many of the same therapeutic interventions found in inpatient treatment.
Both PHPs and IOPs are forms of outpatient care .

Note that an Intensive Outpatient Program (IOP) is a structured addiction treatment option that provides ongoing support through regular therapy sessions while allowing individuals to live at home and maintain daily responsibilities.
Less intensive than PHP, but more intensive than traditional outpatient therapy

Since you won’t be living at the facility, it’s important that the PHP you choose is either relatively close to your home or near other suitable housing, such as outpatient living environments, sober living facilities, Oxford houses, etc.

1 Intensive outpatient programs (IOPs) represent an important level of care on the continuum of treatment options.
Is Standard Outpatient Cheaper?

People who pursue more standard types of outpatient treatment offerings, which could include regularly scheduled individual therapy, group therapy, or a combination of both sessions may only have a time commitment of a few hours a week.
People may continue on with standard outpatient therapy for years, as it best suits their recovery needs.
5 This form of outpatient care may include only individual therapy, only group therapy, or a combination of the two.
Standard outpatient treatment is usually longer in duration than IOP and can continue for months or years.
Because of this, standard outpatient therapy is often cheaper than IOP per week.
Standard outpatient is a fantastic form of care, but it may not be enough to manage all of your symptoms and treatment needs.
, case management) provided in some IOPs have been shown to have better outcomes than those who only received standard outpatient therapy.
A professional evaluation from an addiction expert can help to establish which type of outpatient treatment is best for you at the current time.
Rather than thinking about choosing IOP or standard outpatient, consider IOP and standard outpatient.
Standard outpatient is often the step down from IOP.
As a type of outpatient treatment for drug and alcohol misuse, intensive outpatient programs (IOPs) in Rhode Island offer care similar to that of inpatient programs, just at a lower intensity.

An intensive outpatient program is a structured form of outpatient treatment , which means patients live at home while attending treatment in settings such as community mental health centers, clinics, counselors’ offices, hospitals, and outpatient offices within residential programs.
As such, they offer a higher level of care than traditional outpatient programs and a lower level of care than partial hospitalization programs (PHPs) and inpatient services .
3,4 Once someone completes an IOP, they often transition into traditional outpatient care and/or aftercare.
Its AdCare Detox Rhode Island facilities—located in Warwick, Greenville, and South Kingstown—offer a host of outpatient options, including IOP services.
Additionally, the nearby AdCare in North Kingstown, RI, and AdCare Treatment Hospital in Worcester, MA, include inpatient programs as well as some forms of outpatient care.

Also note that many outpatient rehab programs in Rhode Island treat both substance use disorders as well as co-occurring mental conditions , such as depression, anxiety, bipolar disorder, schizophrenia, attention deficit hyperactivity disorder (ADHD), and personality disorders.

What are partial hospitalization programs (PHP)?

What a partial hospitalization program (PHP) entails.
What is a Partial Hospitalization Program (PHP)?

Partial hospitalization programs (PHP)—aka day treatment—are an intensive and highly structured form of outpatient rehab that allows patients to live at home as opposed to within an inpatient treatment center .
When are Partial Hospitalization Programs Appropriate?
Partial hospitalization programs are often employed following inpatient treatment as a form of continuing care or as a first step in recovery, possibly immediately after detox.

Partial hospitalization programs (PHPs) provide a host of benefits, including psychosocial support and individual treatment, both of which can help patients develop coping and relapse-prevention management strategies.
Many of the therapies and services provided in inpatient care are also available in partial hospitalization programs.
What to Look for in a Partial Hospitalization Program for Drug or Alcohol Addiction
How to Find a Partial Hospitalization Program Near You
PHP costs and payment options.
To understand how a PHP compares to other types of treatment, it helps to look at the levels of care set forth by the American Society of Addiction Medicine (ASAM).
PHP care is lower in intensity than inpatient and residential treatment—but higher than:
(When home environments are challenging, sober living or Oxford house environments may serve as alternative living environments during PHP treatment.
Usually, PHP patients have either completed or don’t require detox , and they don’t need 24-hour supervision to ensure their safety and foster ongoing sobriety.
Treatment providers might refer someone to a PHP if they’re medically stable, motivated to participate in intensive therapy, and able to manage their own basic daily responsibilities.
What Services Are Included in a PHP?
PHP Treatment Objectives for Drug and Alcohol Addiction

What Happens During PHP for Drug or Alcohol Addiction?
If you’ve determined that a PHP is a viable solution for you or a loved one, it’s important to shop around for a quality rehab facility that offers PHPs.

Since you won’t be living at the facility, it’s important that the PHP you choose is either relatively close to your home or near other suitable housing, such as outpatient living environments, sober living facilities, Oxford houses, etc.
Explore online reviews to see what patients are saying about the facility and the PHP.
Also, inquire with the facility to see if it has an alumni program, which can offer valuable support following PHP treatment and throughout recovery.
If you have insurance, determine if the facility offering the PHP is in-network with your provider for the PHP level of care and how much you’ll pay out of pocket for treatment.
How Much Does a PHP for Drug or Alcohol Addiction Cost?
If you have insurance, PHP coverage varies by plan.
How to Pay For PHP Without Insurance Coverage

With facilities scattered across the country, American Addiction Centers (AAC) offers a host of PHP payment options.
Contact American Addiction Centers at (629) 302-1607 to find a PHP near you.
As such, they offer a higher level of care than traditional outpatient programs and a lower level of care than partial hospitalization programs (PHPs) and inpatient services .

rawMentions: Coverage for Suboxone Treatment
Suboxone
Medication-assisted treatment (MAT) is the standard of care for treating opioid use disorders.
Suboxone is FDA-approved for use in medication-assisted treatment of opioid use disorders.

As part of an effective MAT strategy, Suboxone may be used as a part of a larger treatment plan that includes behavioral therapeutic interventions, monitoring, and support, and mental health care (if needed).
There are several different MAT options available to people in the United States, but people may choose Suboxone over other forms of MAT for various reasons.
You can also get a prescription for Suboxone by going to a rehabilitation center or Suboxone clinic that is qualified to treat patients with Suboxone and other forms of MAT.
Find an MAT Rehab Center Near Me
Additionally, various qualified providers who are able to prescribe medications can write prescriptions for Suboxone, making this medication much more accessible than some other forms of MAT.
Suboxone Insurance Coverage & Cost Guide (With & Without Insurance)
Suboxone Insurance Coverage & Cost Guide (With & Without Insurance)
Yes, Suboxone treatment may be covered by your insurance provider.
As a special formulation of buprenorphine, Suboxone has contributed to many peoples’ recovery from opioid use disorders.
Which Health Insurance Plans Cover Suboxone?
Suboxone doctors, clinics, and providers do accept many forms of health insurance, which may offer full or partial coverage for Suboxone treatment .
Common health insurance companies that cover the cost of Suboxone treatment include (but are not limited to):
Suboxone clinic fees may vary based on the provider, the location, and the patient.
What is Suboxone
What is Suboxone?

2,5 Suboxone is a combination of buprenorphine (a long-acting partial opioid agonist) and naloxone (an opioid receptor antagonist) and can be used to stabilize people during detox and withdrawal management as well as for longer-term maintenance therapy for opioid use disorders.

With a strong binding affinity for opioid receptors, buprenorphine can also competitively prohibit other illicit and prescription opioids from attaching to these receptors, blocking them from having their reinforcing euphoric effects on the user to discourage continued misuse while on Suboxone treatment.
Keep in mind that Suboxone does have the potential to cause euphoria in some people.
Naloxone has no effect when Suboxone is taken as directed (i.

, oral routes), but it is included in the formulation as an abuse deterrent, as intentional misuse—such as attempts to dissolve the Suboxone to be injected—can result in the onset of unpleasant withdrawal symptoms.
Why do People Use Suboxone?
People utilize Suboxone as one component in a more comprehensive treatment plan to minimize uncomfortable opioid withdrawal symptoms and control opioid cravings.
By managing withdrawal and controlling cravings, Suboxone can help a person to remain focused on their recovery and prevent them from relapsing.
Suboxone has been shown to be effective at helping people remain abstinent from opioids and improve their treatment outcomes.
What is Suboxone Used For?
Suboxone is a medication used to treat opioid use disorder in the United States.
It is important to wait until you have been abstinent from opioids long enough to be experiencing withdrawal symptoms before starting to take Suboxone.

This generally means staying off opioids for at least 12 hours and up to 48 hours, depending on the type of opioids you were using, since taking Suboxone while you are under the influence of opioids can make withdrawal symptoms begin earlier than they normally would.

Using Suboxone as a long-term maintenance medication encourages you to stay abstinent from opioids, especially since this medication reduces cravings and decreases the potentially rewarding effects of other opioids if you do relapse.
Suboxone film or tablets can ease many symptoms of opioid withdrawal.
Preferences for Suboxone
A range of factors can influence a person’s decision to opt for treatment using Suboxone rather than methadone or naltrexone.
For example, Suboxone tends to offer: 2,5,9,11
In some instances, Suboxone may be more readily accessible than methadone, which is only dispensed in hospital and certified clinic settings.
Medical professionals who have been authorized to provide Suboxone can prescribe it in a greater range of settings, including from their office.
Suboxone is formulated to discourage misuse and diversion.

The opioid effects are relatively weaker than full opioid agonist drugs, and a person is unlikely to experience pronounced feelings or euphoria or intoxication from taking Suboxone, when used as prescribed for the treatment of opioid use disorder.
The risk of overdose on methadone is more than 4 times higher than on Suboxone.
It is unlikely that a person will experience an overdose on Suboxone unless it’s combined with other central nervous system depressants like alcohol or benzodiazepines.
Studies show that buprenorphine, not just the Suboxone formula, has positive recovery outcomes.
Suboxone Costs
How Much is Suboxone Without Insurance?
The cost of Suboxone depends on a variety of factors.
Suboxone costs can be different depending on the provider that you obtain your prescription from, whether it is through a doctor’s office, an inpatient facility, or an outpatient clinic.
Suboxone’s cost without insurance depends on whether you receive the brand name or generic version of Suboxone, as well as your dosage.
However, receiving Suboxone treatment without insurance may involve higher out-of-pocket costs than using health insurance to cover part or all of your Suboxone treatment.
Does Medicaid Cover Suboxone?
Medicaid generally does cover the Suboxone cost for treatment of opioid use disorder, but the extent of coverage depends on various factors.
Suboxone is listed on the preferred drug list for Medicaid in all but one state, although the majority of these states require prior authorization.

Prior authorization means that the medical professional prescribing Suboxone has to obtain approval from Medicaid before the medication will be approved, and may have to submit documentation showing that it is medically necessary, or that the person is receiving counseling in addition to medication.
Medicaid imposes limits on how long a person can stay on Suboxone, although the limit depends on your state and some states set lifetime limits.
If you exceed this lifetime limit, coverage for Suboxone may be denied through Medicaid.
Is There Cost Assistance for Suboxone?
Two cost-assistance programs are available for Suboxone, depending on whether you have private health insurance or no health insurance coverage.
Getting Suboxone
How do I Get Suboxone?
To obtain a prescription for Suboxone, you must find a qualified practitioner who is specially-wavered to prescribe and dispense the medication.
2,7 This practitioner waiver and a modified DEA registration number are provided upon completion of a training program, allowing a provider to prescribe Suboxone.
5,7,11 You may be able to get Suboxone by visiting your family doctor, provided that they possess a buprenorphine waiver.
There is nothing to be ashamed of by asking your family doctor if they can give you a prescription for Suboxone, or discussing the fact that you struggle with opioid addiction.
You can receive Suboxone at American Addiction Centers facilities while receiving evidence-based treatment to address your opioid use disorder.
For more information on how and where to attain Suboxone for the treatment of your opioid use disorder, call American Addiction Centers at
Who Can Prescribe Suboxone?
Suboxone is available through a range of providers and settings.
SAMHSA-certified opioid treatment programs are able to prescribe Suboxone to their patients.
2,5,11,15 You can get Suboxone prescribed by a wavered doctor, physician assistant, or nurse practitioner.
Suboxone: Rational, science, misconceptions .
Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone) .
Rehabs That May Accept Suboxone Insurance
Access to medications such as buprenorphine has proven to be a critical component in managing the opioid addiction crisis our country continues to face.

2,6,7,8 Like other opioid agonist drugs, buprenorphine functions by binding to the body’s opioid receptors; however, as only a partial agonist at these receptors, buprenorphine elicits relatively less pronounced opioid effects when used within prescribed guidelines.
2 Buprenorphine also has a ceiling to its opioid effects, which lowers the risk of overdose.
Buprenorphine may be safer for pregnant and breastfeeding women to use than methadone.

While it can be difficult to track progress over long periods of time, research shows that people who take buprenorphine or methadone are more likely at 18 months to remain abstinent, maintain employment, and engage in 12-step or mutual help meetings to support their recovery .
Buprenorphine is associated with a lower likelihood of dropping out of treatment, relapse, overdose deaths, and improved quality of life vs.
2,9 Opioid maintenance on buprenorphine also is associated with a lower risk of contracting infectious diseases like HIV or hepatitis C, and having new legal issues.
In one study, the majority of people, 75% on buprenorphine were successful in their recovery attempts.
Buprenorphine .
Managing opioid addiction with buprenorphine .
Using Telehealth to support opioid use disorder treatment .
Telehealth services usually allow patients to partake in group and individual therapy, medication-assisted treatment (MAT), peer support groups, and more.

Depending on the program, telehealth may allow you to participate in the same services offered in an in-person setting—such as individual therapy, group therapy, and medication-assisted treatment (MAT)—from the comfort of your home.

evidence.snippet: People utilize Suboxone as one component in a more comprehensive treatment plan to minimize uncomfortable opioid withdrawal symptoms and control opioid cravings.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: By managing withdrawal and controlling cravings, Suboxone can help a person to remain focused on their recovery and prevent them from relapsing.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone has been shown to be effective at helping people remain abstinent from opioids and improve their treatment outcomes.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: What is Suboxone Used For?

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone is a medication used to treat opioid use disorder in the United States.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: It is important to wait until you have been abstinent from opioids long enough to be experiencing withdrawal symptoms before starting to take Suboxone.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: This generally means staying off opioids for at least 12 hours and up to 48 hours, depending on the type of opioids you were using, since taking Suboxone while you are under the influence of opioids can make withdrawal symptoms begin earlier than they normally would.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Using Suboxone as a long-term maintenance medication encourages you to stay abstinent from opioids, especially since this medication reduces cravings and decreases the potentially rewarding effects of other opioids if you do relapse.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone film or tablets can ease many symptoms of opioid withdrawal.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Preferences for Suboxone

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: A range of factors can influence a person’s decision to opt for treatment using Suboxone rather than methadone or naltrexone.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: For example, Suboxone tends to offer: 2,5,9,11

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: In some instances, Suboxone may be more readily accessible than methadone, which is only dispensed in hospital and certified clinic settings.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Medical professionals who have been authorized to provide Suboxone can prescribe it in a greater range of settings, including from their office.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone is formulated to discourage misuse and diversion.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: The opioid effects are relatively weaker than full opioid agonist drugs, and a person is unlikely to experience pronounced feelings or euphoria or intoxication from taking Suboxone, when used as prescribed for the treatment of opioid use disorder.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: The risk of overdose on methadone is more than 4 times higher than on Suboxone.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: It is unlikely that a person will experience an overdose on Suboxone unless it’s combined with other central nervous system depressants like alcohol or benzodiazepines.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Studies show that buprenorphine, not just the Suboxone formula, has positive recovery outcomes.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone Costs

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: How Much is Suboxone Without Insurance?

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: The cost of Suboxone depends on a variety of factors.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone costs can be different depending on the provider that you obtain your prescription from, whether it is through a doctor’s office, an inpatient facility, or an outpatient clinic.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone’s cost without insurance depends on whether you receive the brand name or generic version of Suboxone, as well as your dosage.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: However, receiving Suboxone treatment without insurance may involve higher out-of-pocket costs than using health insurance to cover part or all of your Suboxone treatment.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Does Medicaid Cover Suboxone?

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Medicaid generally does cover the Suboxone cost for treatment of opioid use disorder, but the extent of coverage depends on various factors.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone is listed on the preferred drug list for Medicaid in all but one state, although the majority of these states require prior authorization.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Prior authorization means that the medical professional prescribing Suboxone has to obtain approval from Medicaid before the medication will be approved, and may have to submit documentation showing that it is medically necessary, or that the person is receiving counseling in addition to medication.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Medicaid imposes limits on how long a person can stay on Suboxone, although the limit depends on your state and some states set lifetime limits.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: If you exceed this lifetime limit, coverage for Suboxone may be denied through Medicaid.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Is There Cost Assistance for Suboxone?

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Two cost-assistance programs are available for Suboxone, depending on whether you have private health insurance or no health insurance coverage.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Getting Suboxone

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: How do I Get Suboxone?

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: To obtain a prescription for Suboxone, you must find a qualified practitioner who is specially-wavered to prescribe and dispense the medication.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: 2,7 This practitioner waiver and a modified DEA registration number are provided upon completion of a training program, allowing a provider to prescribe Suboxone.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: 5,7,11 You may be able to get Suboxone by visiting your family doctor, provided that they possess a buprenorphine waiver.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: There is nothing to be ashamed of by asking your family doctor if they can give you a prescription for Suboxone, or discussing the fact that you struggle with opioid addiction.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: You can receive Suboxone at American Addiction Centers facilities while receiving evidence-based treatment to address your opioid use disorder.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: For more information on how and where to attain Suboxone for the treatment of your opioid use disorder, call American Addiction Centers at

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Who Can Prescribe Suboxone?

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Suboxone is available through a range of providers and settings.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: SAMHSA-certified opioid treatment programs are able to prescribe Suboxone to their patients.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: 2,5,11,15 You can get Suboxone prescribed by a wavered doctor, physician assistant, or nurse practitioner.

evidence.snippet: Suboxone: Rational, science, misconceptions .

evidence.snippet: Buprenorphine/naloxone (Suboxone) .

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Rehabs That May Accept Suboxone Insurance

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Access to medications such as buprenorphine has proven to be a critical component in managing the opioid addiction crisis our country continues to face.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: 2,6,7,8 Like other opioid agonist drugs, buprenorphine functions by binding to the body’s opioid receptors; however, as only a partial agonist at these receptors, buprenorphine elicits relatively less pronounced opioid effects when used within prescribed guidelines.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: 2 Buprenorphine also has a ceiling to its opioid effects, which lowers the risk of overdose.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Buprenorphine may be safer for pregnant and breastfeeding women to use than methadone.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: While it can be difficult to track progress over long periods of time, research shows that people who take buprenorphine or methadone are more likely at 18 months to remain abstinent, maintain employment, and engage in 12-step or mutual help meetings to support their recovery .

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: Buprenorphine is associated with a lower likelihood of dropping out of treatment, relapse, overdose deaths, and improved quality of life vs.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: 2,9 Opioid maintenance on buprenorphine also is associated with a lower risk of contracting infectious diseases like HIV or hepatitis C, and having new legal issues.

evidence.label: program:mat
evidence.snippet: In one study, the majority of people, 75% on buprenorphine were successful in their recovery attempts.

rawMentions: Sober Living
Patients well suited to IOPs also have sober living conditions, transportation to and from therapy, and continued motivation to complete the program.
Whether you’re looking for an IOP, telehealth services, outpatient care, detox, or even sober living, American Addiction Centers can help.
Additionally, outpatient care is best suited to those with transportation to attend sessions, living arrangements that support sober living, and the motivation to attend consistently.

Whether you’re looking for an intensive outpatient program in Las Vegas, Nevada or another form of treatment, AAC admissions navigators at can not only help you explore AAC’s treatment centers but also verify your insurance and answer your questions about detox, inpatient, outpatient, sober living, telehealth services, and more.
(When home environments are challenging, sober living or Oxford house environments may serve as alternative living environments during PHP treatment.

Since you won’t be living at the facility, it’s important that the PHP you choose is either relatively close to your home or near other suitable housing, such as outpatient living environments, sober living facilities, Oxford houses, etc.

Additionally, those best suited to an IOP will likely have the continued desire to complete the program as well as reliable transportation to and from sessions, sober living conditions, and a supportive home environment.
